meta.description: Gauntlet-tier SAP-C02 questions: deep architectural decisions, multi-account/multi-region complexity, advanced networking, security tradeoffs, migration strategies. Sudden-death mode.

questions.scenario: A company runs 200 AWS accounts across 5 OUs in AWS Organizations. They want to enforce: (1) no public S3 buckets in any account except a designated 'public-content' OU, (2) only specific approved regions usable, (3) mandatory tagging on resources for cost allocation, (4) prevent disabling of CloudTrail and GuardDuty.
questions.question: Which AWS Organizations feature is the PRIMARY mechanism for these enforcements?
choices: Service Control Policies (SCPs) — applied to root, OUs, or accounts; deny-based; cannot be overridden by IAM permissions; combined with declarative tag policies and Resource Control Policies (RCPs) for resource-level restrictions; pair with AWS Config for detection of non-compliant resources
IAM permission boundaries applied uniformly across every account in the organization — boundaries cap maximum permissions and produce the same org-wide preventive enforcement that SCPs provide
AWS Config conformance packs deployed to every account — Config rules block creation of non-compliant resources at the API call level, which is the standard preventive control across an organization
VPC security groups and NACLs configured org-wide via AWS Network Firewall — these network-layer controls prevent the listed risky actions because every API call traverses the VPC
questions.explanation:
SCPs are AWS Organizations' preventive policy mechanism. Applied at root, OU, or account level; they DENY actions across all principals in the scope (no IAM grant can override an SCP deny). Examples: (1) deny `s3:PutBucketPolicy` and `s3:PutBucketAcl` for actions making buckets public except in `public-content` OU. (2) deny `aws:RequestedRegion` outside approved list. (3) deny resource-creation if mandatory tags missing. (4) deny `cloudtrail:Stop*`, `cloudtrail:Delete*`, `guardduty:Disable*`, `guardduty:Delete*`. Tag Policies (declarative) standardize tag values. Resource Control Policies (RCPs, newer) work like SCPs but apply to specific resource types (S3, SQS, KMS, Secrets Manager) — strengthening cross-account access boundaries. AWS Config detects what slips through; remediation actions or Lambda fix it. Combined with Audit Manager for evidence.

IAM policies (A) operate per-account, can be overridden by other identities. Security groups (C) and WAF (D) are network controls, not preventive policy. The trap: candidates may know SCPs exist but miss RCPs, or confuse SCPs with permission boundaries (which apply to specific IAM principals, not org-wide).
questions.pattern: Multi-account governance hierarchy: Organizations + SCPs (preventive, deny org-wide). RCPs (resource-level deny). Tag Policies (declarative tag standards). AI Service Opt-Out (control AI training data usage). Backup Policies (centralized backup config). Permission Boundaries (cap individual IAM principals). IAM (within-account grants). Layer multiple for defense-in-depth.

questions.scenario: A company's AWS Organization has 300 accounts. Provisioning new accounts takes 2 weeks because each must be configured manually with: networking (VPCs, peering), security baselines (CloudTrail, GuardDuty, Config), IAM Identity Center access, baseline IAM roles, default tags, etc.
questions.question: Which AWS service AUTOMATES this end-to-end with ongoing governance?
choices: AWS Service Catalog with a multi-account product portfolio — Service Catalog provides templated infrastructure including networking, security baselines, and IAM Identity Center wiring as a self-service product
AWS CloudFormation StackSets deployed across the organization — StackSets propagate the same templates to every account and provide the multi-account governance equivalent to Control Tower
AWS Systems Manager Automation runbooks scheduled per-account — SSM runbooks configure the security baselines and networking on each new account when it is created

AWS Control Tower — opinionated multi-account landing zone with pre-built guardrails (mandatory and strongly recommended preventive and detective), Account Factory for standardized account provisioning, integrated with Organizations, Config, CloudTrail, and IAM Identity Center; AFT for IaC-driven customization beyond defaults
questions.explanation:
AWS Control Tower is the opinionated landing-zone service. Provisioning: Account Factory creates new accounts with pre-baked baselines (CloudTrail enabled, Config enabled, GuardDuty optional, IAM Identity Center access, default VPC config, mandatory tags). Guardrails: ~30+ pre-built (mandatory + strongly recommended); detective (Config rules) and preventive (SCPs). Customizable beyond defaults via Account Factory for Terraform (AFT) or Account Factory Customizations. Supports hierarchies of OUs aligned to organizational structure. Integrates with: Organizations (billing + SCPs), Config (compliance), CloudTrail (audit), IAM Identity Center (workforce SSO). For organizations with many accounts, Control Tower reduces provisioning from days to minutes and provides ongoing drift detection.

Option B doesn't scale. Option C is advisory only. Option D is wrong (multi-account is best practice, not single). The trap: candidates may know Organizations but not Control Tower as the automation layer above it.
questions.pattern: Multi-account architecture stack: Organizations (billing + SCPs at the bottom). Control Tower (landing zone, opinionated guardrails). Account Factory / AFT (provisioning). Identity Center (workforce SSO with permission sets). Centralized logging (Org-trail to Log Archive account). Centralized security tooling (Security Hub, GuardDuty, Macie, Detective in Audit account). Service Catalog for self-service infra. AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM) for cross-account sharing.

questions.scenario: A company has a hub-and-spoke network: 1 hub VPC (shared services: DNS, AD, monitoring), 50 spoke VPCs (workloads). They need each spoke to access the hub services and selectively access other spokes. As VPC count grows, peering pairs become unmanageable.
questions.question: Which AWS networking solution scales BEST for this pattern?
choices: AWS Transit Gateway — central regional hub for VPC interconnect (and on-prem via Direct Connect or VPN); route tables on TGW control which VPCs can reach which (segmentation); cross-region peering connects multiple TGWs; replaces full-mesh VPC peering at scale
Consolidate everything into a single VPC with multiple subnets per workload — one VPC simplifies routing and the security-group-per-workload pattern provides the segmentation that TGW would offer
Add VPC peering between every pair (full mesh) and use route tables in each VPC to control which spokes reach which — peering scales linearly with VPC count and supports complex topologies
Use AWS Direct Connect with a private VIF between every pair of VPCs — DX provides the low-latency private connectivity for VPC-to-VPC routing that TGW handles in the AWS network
questions.explanation:
VPC peering doesn't scale: N VPCs need N(N-1)/2 peering connections. 50 spokes = 1225 peerings. AWS Transit Gateway is a regional hub for VPC interconnect: each VPC attaches once to TGW (50 attachments), routes flow through. Multiple TGW route tables enable segmentation: e.g., 'production' route table allows spoke↔hub but not spoke↔spoke; 'shared' route table allows certain spokes to reach others. Cross-region: TGW peering connects TGWs across regions. On-prem: TGW + Direct Connect Gateway + VPN. For very large orgs, Cloud WAN / Network Manager provides higher-level managed networking. Alternatives: shared services VPCs with PrivateLink (purpose-built for service exposure, not generic L3), VPC Lattice (application networking).

Option B doesn't scale. Option C violates blast-radius and security best practices. Option D is wrong (DX is for on-prem connectivity). The trap: candidates may know peering and assume it works for any topology.
questions.pattern: Network topology decision: <5 VPCs ⇒ peering OK. Many VPCs / hub-spoke ⇒ Transit Gateway with route tables for segmentation. Multi-region ⇒ TGW peering or Cloud WAN. Service-to-service exposure (private SaaS, internal APIs) ⇒ PrivateLink endpoints. Application-layer auth/routing ⇒ VPC Lattice. Massive global ⇒ Cloud WAN. Hybrid (on-prem) ⇒ Direct Connect + DX Gateway + TGW.

questions.scenario: A company's central security team in the 'security-tooling' account wants centralized audit logs from every member account: CloudTrail logs, VPC Flow Logs, Config snapshots. Accounts and access must remain segregated (security team can read; member accounts cannot delete logs).
questions.question: Which architecture is CORRECT for centralized log aggregation with separation of duties?
choices: Stream all logs to Amazon CloudWatch Logs in the central account via subscription filters — CloudWatch Logs provides the WORM compliance retention that Object Lock would otherwise add
Each account stores its own CloudTrail logs locally with full delete access; the security team uses cross-account IAM roles to read logs on demand from each account during incident response

Organization Trail (CloudTrail at Org root, applies to all current and future accounts) writing to a centralized S3 bucket in a 'log-archive' account with cross-account write permissions, KMS encryption, S3 Object Lock for compliance retention, Config aggregator for cross-account compliance state, and CloudTrail Lake for SQL-queryable audit data
Use AWS Security Hub as the central log destination — Security Hub aggregates CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and Config snapshots automatically across the organization with built-in tamper protection
questions.explanation:
Centralized log architecture (separation-of-duties model): (1) **Log Archive account** — dedicated, restricted; only ingest + read by security team; member accounts can WRITE but NOT DELETE. (2) **Organization Trail** — single CloudTrail at Org root, automatically captures every account's events (including future accounts), writes to centralized S3 bucket. (3) **S3 bucket policy** — allows writes from all member accounts (via Org ID condition), explicitly denies deletes/overwrites; KMS encryption with key in Audit account. (4) **S3 Object Lock** in Compliance mode — write-once-read-many for regulatory retention (no one can delete during retention period, even root). (5) **Config aggregator** — central read-only view of compliance state across accounts/regions. (6) **VPC Flow Logs** — destination S3 in log-archive (cross-account write). (7) **CloudTrail Lake** — SQL queryable, longer retention, federation across accounts.

Option B violates separation of duties. Options C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may know how to aggregate but miss the deny-delete + Object Lock for tamper-resistance.
questions.pattern: Centralized logging architecture: dedicated Log Archive account (no human users, restricted). Org Trail. Centralized S3 + KMS + Object Lock. Cross-account write (bucket policy). Deny-delete from member accounts. Config aggregator. Security Hub/GuardDuty delegated administrator account for findings. CloudTrail Lake or OpenSearch for query. Splunk/Datadog for SIEM if external.

questions.scenario: A company uses IAM Identity Center for workforce SSO. They need: developers get access to dev/staging accounts as PowerUser, ops gets prod as PowerUser via on-call escalation, finance gets ReadOnly to billing in management account. Different teams have different access patterns.
questions.question: Which IAM Identity Center features fit?
choices: Permission sets (templates: PowerUser, ReadOnly, custom) assigned to user/group + account combinations; integrate with corporate IdP (SAML/SCIM provisioning from Okta/Entra ID); for time-bound elevated access, use IAM Identity Center session policies plus automated workflows (Change Manager or custom break-glass) for temporary elevation on approval

Long-lived IAM users with access keys per developer in each account — rotate keys quarterly and audit usage via CloudTrail; this is the AWS-recommended approach when corporate IdP integration is desired
Cognito user pools provisioned per developer team with cross-account roles — Cognito acts as the workforce IdP and developers assume the appropriate role per account
AWS Directory Service Simple AD with role assignments per user — Simple AD is the AWS-native workforce IdP for federated SSO across accounts and replaces the complexity of Identity Center
questions.explanation:
IAM Identity Center provides workforce SSO across all accounts in the Org. (1) **Permission sets** — reusable IAM templates (e.g., PowerUserAccess, ReadOnlyAccess, custom). (2) **Assignments** — map (user or group, permission set, account) tuples; users authenticate to Identity Center → assume role in target account. (3) **External IdP integration** — SAML 2.0 + SCIM provisioning from Okta, Entra ID (Azure AD), Google Workspace, Ping. Group memberships drive access automatically. (4) **Session duration** — configurable per permission set. (5) **Just-in-time elevated access** — for break-glass / on-call: workflow assigns temporary permission set with limited TTL upon approval (Change Manager, custom Lambda + SNS). (6) **Audit** — CloudTrail logs every assumption.

Option B is the opposite of best practice. Option C is dangerous. Option D doesn't address per-team access. The trap: candidates may default to IAM users when Identity Center is the modern path.
questions.pattern: Workforce identity on AWS: IAM Identity Center as the front door. Federated from corporate IdP. Permission sets per role (don't grant individual permissions ad-hoc). Time-bound elevated access via JIT workflows. Service accounts ⇒ IAM roles (not IAM users). Audit via CloudTrail + Identity Center logs. For machines: roles, not users.

questions.scenario: A company with 50 accounts wants to share a single AWS Config aggregator and use Security Hub centrally. They want findings from every account routed to a central security account for triage.
questions.question: Which AWS architecture pattern is APPROPRIATE?
choices: Aggregate all findings into the management account using AWS CloudTrail Insights — Insights identifies anomalous behavior across all accounts and surfaces unified findings for the security team to triage centrally

Designate a 'security-tooling' (or 'audit') account as delegated administrator for Security Hub, GuardDuty, Macie, Detective, Inspector, and Config aggregator; auto-enable findings/protections for new accounts via Org integration; route critical findings via EventBridge to SNS/PagerDuty/Lambda; combine with AWS Security Lake for centralized OCSF security data
Use AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM) to share each account's GuardDuty findings to the central account; RAM's findings-share feature consolidates the security view without delegated administration

Each account analyzes its own findings independently using local Security Hub instances — the per-account isolation model produces faster triage than centralization because each team owns its own findings
questions.explanation:
AWS security services support delegated administration via Organizations: (1) Designate a 'security-tooling' or 'audit' account as **delegated administrator** for each service — Security Hub, GuardDuty, Macie, Detective, Inspector, Config aggregator. (2) **Auto-enable** for new accounts (Org integration). (3) **Centralized findings** — Security Hub aggregates findings from native AWS services + partner products in OCSF/ASFF format. (4) **EventBridge** routes findings on severity → SNS, PagerDuty, Slack, Lambda for automated remediation. (5) **AWS Security Lake** — centralized data lake of security data across accounts/sources, OCSF format, queryable with Athena/QuickSight. (6) **Config aggregator** — single read-only view of resource compliance org-wide. (7) **CloudTrail Lake** for cross-account audit query. (8) Don't put security tooling in the management account — keep it in a dedicated account for blast-radius isolation.

Option B doesn't centralize. Options C, D don't scale. The trap: candidates may know individual security services but not the delegated-admin pattern that makes them work org-wide.
questions.pattern: Centralized security posture: delegated admin account (dedicated, not management). Security Hub (findings aggregator). GuardDuty (threat detection). Macie (PII discovery in S3). Detective (security investigations). Inspector (vuln scanning). Config aggregator (compliance state). Security Lake (raw security data). EventBridge (orchestrate response). Auto-enable for new accounts.

questions.scenario: A company has 100 accounts and wants to share resources cross-account: Transit Gateways for networking, License Manager licenses, Glue Data Catalog tables, EC2 AMIs, VPC subnets, RDS Aurora DB clusters.
questions.question: Which AWS service is PURPOSE-BUILT for centralized cross-account resource sharing?
choices: Email IAM credentials and resource ARNs to each account owner — each account's owner imports the resource into their own account using the AWS CLI and the shared ARN

Use S3 Cross-Region Replication for everything — CRR replicates all shared AWS resources including Transit Gateways, License Manager licenses, and Glue Data Catalog tables across accounts automatically

AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM) — share resources with specific accounts, OUs, or the entire Organization; supports many resource types (Transit Gateway, subnets, License Manager, Route 53 Resolver rules, Glue Data Catalog, EC2 Capacity Reservations, ACM Private CA, Outposts, Service Catalog portfolios); shared resources are not duplicated

Configure VPC peering with shared resource discovery enabled — peering's resource-share extension exposes Transit Gateway routes, Glue tables, and License Manager configurations across peered accounts
questions.explanation:
AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM) is the cross-account resource-sharing service. Resource owner shares a resource (or set) with specific accounts, OUs, or the whole Org via Resource Share. Recipients see and use the resource without copies. Supported types include: Transit Gateways, VPC subnets, Route 53 Resolver rules, License Manager configurations, Glue Data Catalog databases/tables, AWS Network Firewall policies, EC2 Capacity Reservations, On-Demand Capacity Reservations, ACM Private CA, Outposts, Service Catalog portfolios, Resolver query log configurations, and many more (~40 types). RAM eliminates resource duplication and centralizes management. Pair with Organizations for share-with-OU (auto-include new accounts).

Option B is for S3 replication, not multi-resource sharing. Option C is networking-only. Option D is absurd. The trap: candidates may not know RAM supports the breadth they need; they may try to replicate resources.
questions.pattern: Cross-account sharing strategies by resource: RAM (Transit Gateway, subnets, Glue Catalog, License Manager, etc.). Resource policies (S3 buckets, KMS keys, Lambda functions, SQS, SNS — natively support cross-account access). Cross-account IAM roles (general-purpose). For Bedrock Custom Models: RAM. For SageMaker Model Registry packages: resource policies. For ECR images: repository policies.

questions.scenario: A company has a workload spanning 3 AWS accounts (frontend, backend, data) plus on-premises. They need encrypted communication between all components without each developer building TLS manually.
questions.question: Which AWS service provides MUTUAL TLS / SERVICE-MESH-LIKE capabilities for AWS workloads?
choices: Use Application Load Balancer with mTLS authentication across all microservices — ALB's mTLS feature handles the cross-account encryption and the listener rules enforce service-to-service authentication

Configure AWS Global Accelerator with TLS termination at the edge for all internal services — Global Accelerator's anycast network provides the cross-account encrypted communication path between microservices

AWS Direct Connect with private VIFs between accounts and Site-to-Site VPN as backup — DX provides the encrypted backbone connectivity that powers cross-account mTLS, with private VIFs handling per-VPC isolation

Amazon VPC Lattice for cross-account/cross-VPC service-to-service connectivity with IAM/SigV4 auth, encryption, and observability; AWS App Mesh (deprecated) for traditional Envoy-based service mesh on ECS/EKS/EC2; AWS PrivateLink (Interface VPC Endpoints) for service exposure between VPCs/accounts via TLS
questions.explanation:
Multiple options for service-to-service connectivity with encryption: (1) **AWS App Mesh** — Envoy-based service mesh, control plane managed by AWS; provides mTLS, retries, traffic shaping, observability; works with ECS, EKS, EC2. Note: App Mesh is being deprecated in favor of VPC Lattice. (2) **Amazon VPC Lattice** (newer, recommended) — application networking layer; cross-VPC and cross-account service-to-service connectivity with IAM/SigV4 auth, TLS encryption, observability built in; fits the multi-account use case naturally. (3) **AWS PrivateLink** — service exposure via Interface VPC Endpoints; private TLS-encrypted, no need for VPC peering or routing; great for SaaS-style consumption. Choose by abstraction level: PrivateLink for service exposure, VPC Lattice for application-layer connectivity, App Mesh (legacy) for traditional service mesh needs.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may know one but not the latest options or when to use which.
questions.pattern: Connectivity layers: L3/4 — VPC peering, TGW, Cloud WAN. PrivateLink — service-to-service via endpoints (TLS, no peering). VPC Lattice — application networking with auth/observability across accounts. App Mesh (deprecated) — Envoy mesh. API Gateway / ALB / NLB — public/internal app entry points. Choose abstraction level matching needs.

questions.scenario: A company has on-prem datacenter with 10 Gbps existing connectivity to AWS via a single Direct Connect connection. They need: redundancy (single DX is a single point of failure), MACsec encryption for the link, and connectivity to multiple AWS regions.
questions.question: Which architecture is APPROPRIATE for resilient hybrid networking?
choices: Site-to-Site VPN over the public internet as the only connectivity — modern AWS VPN delivers the same throughput as Direct Connect with built-in encryption, eliminating the need for DX or MACsec
AWS Global Accelerator endpoints fronting the on-prem datacenter — Global Accelerator's anycast routing across multiple AWS regions provides the redundancy and encryption that DX would otherwise need
Single Direct Connect connection with no backup — a single 10 Gbps connection has the SLA needed for production hybrid networking and adding a second connection introduces unnecessary BGP complexity

Two Direct Connect connections at different DX locations (different cable paths and different AWS edge devices), each terminated to a Direct Connect Gateway for cross-region routing; MACsec on supported port speeds (10G/100G) for in-transit link encryption; BGP for failover; backup Site-to-Site VPN over internet as third tier
questions.explanation:
Direct Connect resiliency tiers (AWS Resiliency Recommendations): (1) **Development & test** — single DX (single AZ, single device). (2) **High resiliency** — 2 DX connections at separate DX locations, terminated at separate AWS devices (different fault domains). (3) **Maximum resiliency** — 4 DX connections across 2+ DX locations, each connection at separate edge devices. Combine with: (4) **Direct Connect Gateway** — single global entity routing between DX and multiple AWS regions / VPCs (or TGWs). (5) **MACsec** — link-layer encryption (IEEE 802.1AE) on supported port speeds (10/100 Gbps); requires compatible customer-edge devices. (6) **Backup VPN over internet** — additional fallback (Site-to-Site VPN with BGP). (7) **BGP** for automatic failover. For mission-critical, fall back to a second SP at the colocation level too.

Option B has no redundancy. Option C is lower-bandwidth/higher-latency only. Option D is absurd. The trap: candidates may know DX exists but not the resiliency framework or MACsec specifics.
questions.pattern: Hybrid connectivity tiers: Site-to-Site VPN (cheap, internet-routed, encrypted, ~Gbps). Direct Connect (private, 1/10/100 Gbps, lower latency). DX + DX Gateway (multi-region routing). MACsec (link encryption on supported speeds). DX with VPN backup (best of both). Cloud WAN for very large global. Always plan for failure: separate paths, separate DX locations, BGP-driven failover.

questions.scenario: A company runs Active Directory on-premises. AWS workloads need to authenticate users to AD without exposing AD to the internet, and Linux EC2 instances need to join the AD domain.
questions.question: Which AWS service is PURPOSE-BUILT for AD integration?
choices: Use Amazon Cognito User Pools as the workforce identity store — Cognito's federation feature integrates with on-prem AD via SAML and replaces the need for Directory Service in AWS workloads

AWS Directory Service — AWS Managed Microsoft AD (managed AD that can establish a trust with on-prem AD), AD Connector (proxy to on-prem AD without managing AD in AWS), Simple AD (low-cost, basic features); Linux EC2 instances join via SSM Domain Join or seamless join; pair with FSx for Windows for AD-integrated file shares; use private connectivity (VPN/DX)
Configure AWS Lambda functions that proxy authentication requests to on-prem AD — Lambda's auth-proxy pattern eliminates the need for Directory Service while keeping AD on-prem
Just use IAM users in each AWS account — IAM is the AWS replacement for AD and provides authentication for all workloads including Linux EC2 instances and SQL Server Windows Authentication
questions.explanation:
AWS Directory Service options: (1) **AWS Managed Microsoft AD** — fully managed Microsoft AD; supports trusts (one-way or two-way) with on-prem AD; multi-AZ; can be schema-extended; supports AD-aware AWS services (RDS for SQL Server with Windows Auth, FSx for Windows, WorkSpaces, etc.). (2) **AD Connector** — proxy/redirector to on-prem AD; no AD in AWS; lightweight; auth flows back to on-prem; requires connectivity (VPN/DX). (3) **Simple AD** — small, low-cost (Samba-based), limited; small workloads only. EC2 instances join the domain via SSM Domain Join automation document or seamless join. FSx for Windows File Server provides SMB shares with NTFS ACLs, integrated with AD. Use private connectivity (VPN/DX) — never expose AD to internet.

Option B replaces AD, doesn't integrate. Option C is irrelevant. Option D is wrong. The trap: candidates may know IAM but not the directory-services side for Windows workloads.
questions.pattern: AD integration patterns: AWS Managed Microsoft AD (fully in AWS, can trust on-prem). AD Connector (proxy to on-prem). One-way trust: resources in AWS, identities in on-prem. Two-way trust: bidirectional. EC2 Domain Join via SSM. FSx Windows + Managed AD for SMB. RDS SQL Server / Oracle Windows Auth via Managed AD. WorkSpaces auth via Managed AD or AD Connector. For workforce SSO across SaaS + AWS: IAM Identity Center federated from AD.

questions.scenario: A company has 20 AWS accounts that all need access to a central Glue Data Catalog with data governance: column-level access, tag-based access, audit. Direct access to underlying S3 must be controlled too.
questions.question: Which AWS service architecture fits?
choices: Make the central S3 bucket public with bucket policies restricting access by source IP and source VPC — the IP-based filter provides the column-level governance equivalent to Lake Formation

AWS Lake Formation deployed in a central 'data-lake' account with Glue Data Catalog as the metastore; cross-account sharing via Lake Formation grants and AWS RAM; consumers query via Athena, Redshift Spectrum, EMR, Glue jobs, or SageMaker — all enforce Lake Formation column/row/tag-based permissions; underlying S3 access governed via Lake Formation Storage policies; auditable via CloudTrail
Use Amazon DataZone subscriptions across accounts — DataZone's column-level access model directly enforces per-column permissions in the underlying S3 data without requiring Lake Formation
Each account maintains its own copy of the data with S3 Cross-Region Replication keeping them synchronized; per-account IAM policies enforce column-level access controls in the local copy
questions.explanation:
AWS Lake Formation provides fine-grained data governance over data in S3 + Glue Data Catalog: (1) **Centralized catalog** — one Glue Data Catalog in 'data-lake' account; databases + tables defined here. (2) **Cross-account sharing** — Lake Formation grants permissions to specific accounts/users/groups; RAM under the hood for share distribution. (3) **Column-level access** — exclude sensitive columns from specific principals. (4) **Row-level filters** — data filters with predicates. (5) **Tag-based access (LF-Tags)** — assign tags to resources, grant access by tag. (6) **Storage policies** — Lake Formation manages S3 access via service-linked role; consumers don't need direct S3 IAM permissions. (7) **Engines** — Athena, Redshift Spectrum, EMR (Spark, Hive), Glue jobs, SageMaker all enforce Lake Formation perms uniformly. (8) **Audit** — every query attempt logged; data access events visible.

Option B duplicates data, breaks consistency. Options C, D are absurd. The trap: candidates may know S3 + IAM but miss the data-governance layer.
questions.pattern: Data lake architecture: S3 (storage). Glue Data Catalog (metadata). Lake Formation (governance, fine-grained access, cross-account). Engines: Athena (ad hoc SQL), Redshift Spectrum (warehouse + lake), EMR (Spark/Hive/Presto), Glue (ETL), SageMaker (ML). Audit: CloudTrail + Lake Formation events. PII scanning: Macie. DataZone for catalog/discovery layer.

questions.scenario: A company is consolidating 100 separate AWS accounts (each owned by a different team) into AWS Organizations for centralized billing and governance. Some accounts have existing resources, IAM users, S3 buckets — they need to be invited or created without breaking workloads.
questions.question: Which AWS Organizations practices fit for the migration?
choices: Create a new clean management account; INVITE existing accounts to the Org (they accept and join, retaining all resources); structure into OUs by environment or BU based on governance needs; apply minimal SCPs initially to avoid breaking workloads; enable consolidated billing immediately for cost benefits; layer on guardrails (Control Tower, Security Hub, Config) gradually
Combine all existing accounts into a single AWS account using AWS Resource Access Manager — RAM's account-merge feature consolidates resources without rebuilding workloads

Skip Organizations entirely and continue with independent accounts — the consolidated-billing benefit is offset by the loss of per-account autonomy and Organizations introduces complexity for the small benefit
Force every existing account to be deleted and rebuilt from scratch within the new Organization — clean rebuilds eliminate inherited misconfigurations and produce a uniform baseline across accounts
questions.explanation:
Account consolidation into AWS Organizations: (1) **Management account** — create a new clean account dedicated to billing+governance (don't repurpose an existing workload account). (2) **Invitation flow** — for existing accounts, send invitation; the account's owner accepts. They retain all resources, IAM users, etc. (3) **OU structure** — organize by environment (Dev/Staging/Prod) or BU; structure should be GOVERNANCE-driven (which SCPs apply where), not just hierarchical aesthetics. (4) **Phased SCPs** — start permissive; add deny rules iteratively after observing behavior; avoid breaking existing workloads. (5) **Consolidated billing** activates immediately — Reserved Instances and Savings Plans share across accounts; volume discounts. (6) **Gradual hardening** — layer Control Tower (or DIY landing zone), Security Hub, GuardDuty, Config rules. (7) **Rare**: workload migration for re-organization (e.g., per-environment accounts that didn't exist before). (8) **Move accounts between Orgs** — possible but requires removal + invitation, brief downtime.

Option B is destructive. Options C, D miss the value. The trap: candidates may not know SCPs apply only after invitation acceptance.
questions.pattern: Account consolidation playbook: management account (clean, no workloads). Invite existing. OU by governance need. Permissive SCPs first. Consolidated billing wins. Layer guardrails gradually. Centralize logging + security tooling in dedicated accounts. AFT or Service Catalog for new account provisioning.

questions.scenario: A company runs Aurora PostgreSQL clusters in multiple AWS accounts. They want centralized observability without DBAs needing access to all accounts.
questions.question: Which approach fits CENTRALIZED MONITORING WITH ACCOUNT BOUNDARIES?
choices: Give DBAs IAM permissions in every account directly so they can view metrics natively — direct access provides the lowest latency and avoids the cross-account observability latency overhead
Configure VPC peering between every account's monitoring VPC and the central account so the central CloudWatch agent can scrape metrics from every database directly via the peered network

Cross-account observability with Amazon CloudWatch — designate a central monitoring account, source accounts share metrics/logs/traces via CloudWatch Cross-Account Observability links; CloudWatch Logs cross-account subscription filters; centralized dashboards; integrate with managed services like Aurora Performance Insights and Database Activity Streams; AWS Security Lake for SIEM-style aggregation
Schedule a Lambda function in each account that exports CloudWatch metrics to a central S3 bucket every minute; central QuickSight dashboards visualize the metrics from S3 for unified observability
questions.explanation:
Centralized observability without granting full account access: (1) **CloudWatch Cross-Account Observability** — sink account observes source accounts; share metrics, logs, traces; central dashboards; principals in sink account read but don't get broad access to source accounts. (2) **CloudWatch Logs cross-account subscription** — stream logs from source accounts to central destination (Kinesis Streams, Firehose, OpenSearch). (3) **Aurora Performance Insights** — managed metrics dashboard for DB performance; can be viewed across accounts via cross-account observability. (4) **Database Activity Streams** — near-real-time stream of all DB activity (queries, logins, etc.) to Kinesis; consumable by central security tools. (5) **AWS Security Lake** — for security-related telemetry (CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, Route 53 Resolver query logs) across accounts in OCSF format. Combine to give DBAs+ops the visibility they need without breaking account isolation.

Option B violates separation. Options C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may not know about cross-account observability (relatively new).
questions.pattern: Multi-account observability: CloudWatch Cross-Account Observability (metrics, logs, traces). CloudWatch Logs subscription (cross-account streaming). X-Ray cross-account (distributed tracing). Security Lake (security data). Database Activity Streams (RDS/Aurora). Centralize while preserving account isolation. For external SIEM (Splunk, Datadog): forwarders in each account → central destination.

questions.scenario: A company is designing a new global, latency-sensitive web application. Users in NA, EU, APAC must see <200ms response. The application has dynamic API responses (uncacheable) and static assets (cacheable).
questions.question: Which AWS architecture provides GLOBAL LOW LATENCY for both static and dynamic content?
choices: CloudFront in front of S3 origins for static assets (long TTL) and ALB or API Gateway in multiple regions for dynamic APIs; CloudFront uses edge locations for caching plus dynamic acceleration via persistent connections, TCP optimizations, and Origin Shield; Route 53 latency-based routing or Global Accelerator for non-CloudFront-fronted endpoints; API Gateway Edge-Optimized vs Regional based on global vs regional patterns
Use CloudFront for everything with no origin — modern CloudFront includes built-in compute and storage at the edge that eliminates the need for backing services in any region

Deploy Route 53 health checks against on-premises servers and let DNS routing handle global latency — Route 53's per-region latency intelligence routes users to the lowest-latency origin without needing CloudFront

Single region in us-east-1 with provisioned-throughput-mode RDS — modern AWS networking makes single-region deployments performant globally and CloudFront's edge caching alone covers the latency budget
questions.explanation:
Global latency architecture for mixed content: (1) **CloudFront** — global CDN with 600+ edge POPs; serves cached static content from edge (S3 origin with long TTL). (2) **Dynamic content acceleration** — even uncached requests benefit from CloudFront via persistent connections to origin, TCP optimizations, Origin Shield (regional cache layer in front of origin), and AWS backbone routing (faster than public internet). (3) **Multi-region origins** — for dynamic APIs, deploy ALBs/API Gateways in multiple regions; CloudFront origin failover or origin selection based on geography. (4) **Route 53 latency routing** — for non-CloudFront endpoints, route to nearest healthy region. (5) **Global Accelerator** — TCP/UDP anycast for non-HTTP workloads or for dynamic content where you want anycast directly to your endpoint. (6) **API Gateway** — Edge-Optimized (CloudFront-fronted automatically) vs Regional + custom CloudFront. (7) **Aurora Global Database** for global data; DynamoDB Global Tables; ElastiCache Global Datastore for cross-region cache.

Option B fails latency. Options C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may know CloudFront but miss its dynamic-content acceleration value.
questions.pattern: Global low-latency stack: CloudFront (static + dynamic acceleration). Multi-region origins. Route 53 latency-based routing or Global Accelerator (anycast). Aurora Global / DynamoDB Global / Global Datastore for data. WAF + Shield Advanced at the edge. Origin Shield for cache optimization.

questions.scenario: A company is designing a new high-throughput event-driven backend: ingest 1M events/sec, process with low latency, fan out to multiple consumers, occasionally replay events for debugging or new consumers.
questions.question: Which AWS service is APPROPRIATE for the ingestion + replay capability?
choices: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams — durable ordered log of records, retention 1-365 days, multiple consumers via shards (classic) or enhanced fan-out, replay from any sequence/timestamp; alternatives include Amazon MSK (Kafka, more flexibility, more ops) or Amazon EventBridge (event routing with limited replay via Archive feature)
Amazon SQS Standard queue with 14-day retention and a long-poll consumer model — SQS supports replay through the message-redrive feature and handles 1M events/sec via auto-scaling
Amazon S3 with event notifications and S3 Object Versioning — versioning provides the replay capability by allowing consumers to reprocess any past version of any object
Amazon DynamoDB with DynamoDB Streams enabled — Streams retains 24 hours of changes which is sufficient for replay and DynamoDB scales to millions of writes per second on-demand
questions.explanation:
**Amazon Kinesis Data Streams** is purpose-built for high-throughput event ingestion with replay: (1) **Durable ordered log** — within a shard, records ordered. (2) **Throughput** — millions of records/sec with adequate sharding. (3) **Retention** — 1 day default, up to 365 days. (4) **Replay** — consumers checkpoint via Kinesis Client Library or Lambda event-source mappings; can re-position to any point in retention window. (5) **Multiple consumers** — classic shard reads (5 reads/sec/shard total) or enhanced fan-out (per-consumer 2 MB/sec/shard). (6) **Use cases** — real-time analytics, ML feature pipelines, log aggregation. **Amazon MSK** (managed Kafka) is similar with more flexibility (consumer groups, exactly-once with transactions) at higher operational complexity. **EventBridge** is event-driven but optimized for routing/transformation, with limited replay (Archive + Replay feature for replays of archived events).

Option B (SQS) doesn't allow replay; messages once consumed are gone. Option C (DynamoDB Streams) is per-table, lower throughput, 24-hour retention. Option D (S3 events) is for object events, not streaming. The trap: candidates may default to SQS because it's familiar, missing the replay requirement.
questions.pattern: Event-system selection: Kinesis Streams (high-throughput streaming with replay). MSK (Kafka, more flexibility, more ops). EventBridge (event-driven routing, schema registry, archive/replay). SQS (work queues, no replay). SNS (pub/sub fan-out, no replay). Kinesis Firehose (managed delivery to S3/Redshift/etc., no replay). Choose by throughput, replay, ordering, and ecosystem fit.

questions.scenario: A company designs a new microservices architecture on EKS. They need: service-to-service mTLS, secrets management without storing in pods, runtime workload identity for AWS APIs, scale-to-zero for ad-hoc workloads.
questions.question: Which AWS-native EKS patterns apply?
choices: IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) for workload identity (or EKS Pod Identity, the newer alternative); Secrets Manager / Parameter Store integration via CSI driver or external-secrets controller; mTLS via VPC Lattice for service-to-service; KEDA / Karpenter for scaling (Karpenter for nodes, KEDA for pods including scale-to-zero)

Run a single privileged pod per node that owns all AWS credentials and proxies API calls for application pods on the same node — this pattern centralizes IAM management and reduces per-pod credential complexity

Hardcode AWS access keys in Kubernetes ConfigMaps that pods read at startup — ConfigMaps are encrypted at rest in etcd and IAM access keys with quarterly rotation provide adequate security for EKS workloads

Use Cognito Identity Pools to issue temporary AWS credentials to each pod via the EKS cluster's API server — Cognito is the AWS-native workforce identity service and works for any compute including Kubernetes pods
questions.explanation:
Modern EKS production stack: (1) **Workload identity** — IRSA maps Kubernetes service accounts to IAM roles via OIDC; pod assumes role automatically. EKS Pod Identity (newer) simplifies this further with EKS-native auth. Replaces dangerous patterns of putting AWS keys in containers or relying on broad node instance profiles. (2) **Secrets** — never bake into images. AWS Secrets Manager + Parameter Store accessible via CSI driver (mount as files) or external-secrets-operator (sync to K8s secrets); KMS-encrypted at rest, IAM-controlled. (3) **mTLS / service mesh** — VPC Lattice for app networking with auth (newer, recommended). App Mesh exists but deprecated. Istio is open-source alternative. (4) **Scaling** — Karpenter for node provisioning (replaces Cluster Autoscaler), much faster scaling; KEDA for pod scaling on custom metrics including scale-to-zero. (5) **Networking** — AWS VPC CNI for pod networking; pods get VPC IPs.

Options B, C, D are anti-patterns. The trap: candidates may know one piece but miss the integrated stack.
questions.pattern: EKS production stack: identity (IRSA / Pod Identity). Secrets (Secrets Manager + CSI / external-secrets). Networking (VPC CNI, optional Cilium). Service mesh (VPC Lattice or Istio). Scaling (Karpenter for nodes, KEDA for pods). Observability (CloudWatch Container Insights, Prometheus, Grafana). Security (Falco, Inspector, image scanning in ECR). GitOps (ArgoCD, Flux).

questions.scenario: A company designs a high-volume transactional system: 50K writes/sec, single-digit-ms latency, multi-region active-active (writes from any region), durability.
questions.question: Which AWS data store is APPROPRIATE?
choices: Aurora single-region cluster with read replicas in other regions — Aurora's read replicas can be promoted to writers in any region within seconds, providing the active-active behavior with relational guarantees

Self-managed MySQL on EC2 with Multi-AZ deployment and cross-region replication via binlog — this configuration delivers the active-active multi-region writes that DynamoDB Global Tables provides at lower cost

Amazon DynamoDB with Global Tables — multi-region active-active with single-digit ms latency, scales to >10M ops/sec, durability via 3-AZ replication per region plus cross-region async replication; last-write-wins conflict resolution by default, or optimistic concurrency via condition expressions for stricter semantics; no manual sharding

Amazon S3 with Multi-Region Access Points and randomized object keys — MRAP routes writes to the nearest region and S3's eventual consistency model handles the active-active conflict resolution automatically
questions.explanation:
DynamoDB Global Tables is purpose-built for global, low-latency, active-active write scenarios: (1) **Multi-region active-active** — write to any region; replicates to other regions asynchronously (typically <1s). (2) **Performance** — single-digit ms reads/writes at any scale; 'on-demand' or 'provisioned' capacity. (3) **Durability** — 3-AZ replication within each region + cross-region async. (4) **Conflict resolution** — last-write-wins by timestamp (default); or use optimistic concurrency via condition expressions for stricter semantics. (5) **No sharding** — DynamoDB shards transparently. (6) **Streams** — DynamoDB Streams for change capture; pair with Lambda for downstream processing. **Aurora Global Database** offers global reads + cross-region failover but writes are typically single-region (Aurora Limitless / Aurora DSQL change this for some scenarios; check current capabilities). Aurora is fine for transactional + relational workloads at this scale, but Global Tables is the active-active winner.

Option B (single-region) misses multi-region requirement. Option C doesn't scale. Option D is for objects, not transactions. The trap: candidates may not know Global Tables is true active-active.
questions.pattern: Multi-region data stores: DynamoDB Global Tables (active-active, NoSQL). Aurora Global Database (active-passive primary writes, global reads). Aurora DSQL (newer, multi-region active-active SQL — check current GA status). S3 Multi-Region Access Points (object). ElastiCache Global Datastore (Redis, active-passive). MemoryDB Multi-Region. Choose by data model, conflict tolerance, latency.

questions.scenario: A company designs a new system requiring a relational database with: ACID transactions, complex SQL, 100 TB+ data, predictable performance, automated failover, point-in-time recovery, easy scaling beyond a single instance.
questions.question: Which AWS relational option is MOST APPROPRIATE?
choices: Self-managed PostgreSQL on EC2 with custom replication scripts — self-management offers more flexibility than RDS and the team's existing PostgreSQL expertise translates directly to EC2
Amazon Redshift with Aurora-compatible mode — Redshift's relational extension handles ACID transactions and complex SQL at petabyte scale without the storage limits of Aurora

Amazon Aurora (PostgreSQL or MySQL compatible) — distributed storage layer separating compute from storage (scales to 128/256 TiB+), 6-way replication across 3 AZs, automated failover, backtrack/PITR, read replicas with millisecond replication lag, Serverless v2 for elastic compute, Aurora Limitless for sharded write scaling
Amazon DynamoDB always — relational databases are obsolete in cloud-native architectures and DynamoDB's transactions feature handles all ACID requirements at any scale
questions.explanation:
Amazon Aurora is the AWS-native relational service designed for cloud scale: (1) **Storage** — distributed, separated from compute, scales automatically to multiple TiB; 6-way replication across 3 AZs. (2) **Compute** — primary writer + up to 15 read replicas (sub-10ms lag); promote any replica to primary in seconds for failover. (3) **Backups** — continuous to S3, PITR within retention. (4) **Backtrack** (MySQL flavor) — rewind cluster to a prior point quickly. (5) **Aurora Serverless v2** — auto-scaling compute (ACU-based, fast scale up/down). (6) **Aurora Limitless** — sharded write scaling (single logical DB, transparent sharding). (7) **Global Database** — cross-region reads + DR. (8) **Zero-ETL** to Redshift / OpenSearch. Multi-AZ + read replicas + Serverless gives the agility this scenario needs without DB management overhead.

Option B is wrong (relational has clear use cases). Option C reintroduces management burden. Option D is absurd. The trap: candidates may default to RDS PostgreSQL/MySQL (fine, but Aurora is the cloud-native upgrade for AWS-native scenarios).
questions.pattern: Relational on AWS spectrum: RDS (managed Postgres/MySQL/Oracle/SQL Server/MariaDB — traditional, single-instance with replicas). Aurora (cloud-native, distributed storage, faster failover, more scaling). Aurora Serverless v2 (auto-scale compute). Aurora Limitless (sharded writes). Aurora Global (multi-region). For OLAP: Redshift. For mix: zero-ETL.

questions.scenario: A company is designing a new search/analytics workload: index 10M docs/day, sub-second search latency, full-text + vector + structured filtering, dashboards.
questions.question: Which AWS service is PURPOSE-BUILT for this?
choices: Amazon RDS with LIKE queries and pgvector extension — RDS scales to 10M docs/day for full-text and vector search in a single managed Postgres instance
Amazon S3 with grep via Athena queries — Athena's full-text and vector-search functions provide the sub-second search latency at any document volume

Amazon OpenSearch Service — distributed search engine, full-text plus structured filtering plus vector kNN search, OpenSearch Dashboards for visualization, ML plugins (anomaly detection, forecasting, clustering), Security Analytics for SIEM use cases, OpenSearch Serverless for variable workloads (also used as a vector store backing Bedrock Knowledge Bases)
Amazon DynamoDB with full-text search enabled — DynamoDB's secondary indexes handle full-text and vector search at sub-second latency for any analytics workload
questions.explanation:
Amazon OpenSearch Service is the managed search-and-analytics service: (1) **Indexing** — millions of docs/day; sharding + replication for performance and durability. (2) **Search** — full-text (BM25), vector kNN (HNSW), hybrid, structured filters, aggregations, geo. (3) **Dashboards** — OpenSearch Dashboards (Kibana fork) for visualization. (4) **Analytics** — aggregations, sub-second response on terabytes. (5) **OpenSearch ML** — anomaly detection, forecasting, clustering plugins. (6) **Security Analytics** — SIEM-like detector rules. (7) **Vector** — supported as a vector store backing Bedrock Knowledge Bases for RAG. (8) **Serverless** — auto-scaling for variable workloads (different pricing model than provisioned). (9) **Use cases** — log analytics, observability, e-commerce search, RAG vector store, security analytics.

Option B is wrong domain. Options C, D are absurd at scale. The trap: candidates may know Elasticsearch generically but not realize OpenSearch is the AWS-managed service.
questions.pattern: Search and analytics: OpenSearch (full-text + vector + analytics). Athena (SQL on S3). Redshift (warehouse). Kendra (intelligent enterprise search with semantic ranking). For RAG vector store: OpenSearch Serverless, Aurora pgvector, S3 Vectors, Pinecone, etc. Dashboards: OpenSearch Dashboards, QuickSight, Grafana.

questions.scenario: A company designs a new data warehouse for BI reporting on 50 TB of historical data + real-time updates from 100s of source systems. Analysts will run complex SQL with joins.
questions.question: Which AWS data warehouse design fits?
choices: Use Amazon DynamoDB with global secondary indexes — DynamoDB's analytical query mode handles complex SQL with joins at petabyte scale and is the AWS-recommended warehouse

Amazon Redshift — columnar, massively parallel SQL warehouse; Redshift Serverless (auto-scaling) for variable load; load via COPY from S3 (Parquet, batch) and Streaming Ingestion (from Kinesis/MSK for real-time); Redshift Spectrum for querying S3 without loading; zero-ETL from Aurora/RDS/DynamoDB; integrates with Glue Data Catalog and Lake Formation; Redshift ML for in-database ML
Use Amazon Athena with the Glue Data Catalog as the data warehouse — Athena's SQL on S3 handles 50 TB at sub-second latency and replaces the need for Redshift entirely
Run BI queries directly against the transactional databases — modern databases handle mixed OLTP and OLAP workloads, and routing analyst queries to the source removes the ETL overhead
questions.explanation:
Amazon Redshift is the AWS data warehouse: (1) **Architecture** — columnar storage, massively parallel processing (MPP) compute. (2) **Provisioned vs Serverless** — Redshift Serverless auto-scales compute (RPU-based); Provisioned for predictable steady workloads. (3) **Loading** — COPY from S3 (Parquet, ORC, CSV), Streaming Ingestion (direct from Kinesis Streams / MSK to materialized views, sub-second latency for streaming data). (4) **Federated queries** — Redshift Spectrum queries S3 data via Glue Data Catalog without loading; Federated Query into RDS/Aurora live tables. (5) **Zero-ETL** — Aurora MySQL/PostgreSQL, RDS for MySQL, DynamoDB integrations stream changes to Redshift continuously, no custom ETL. (6) **Lake Formation** — fine-grained access control. (7) **Redshift ML** — train + infer ML models with SQL (uses SageMaker under the hood). (8) **Data Sharing** — share data across Redshift clusters / accounts without copying.

Option B kills transactional performance. Options C, D are wrong scale/model. The trap: candidates may know Redshift exists but not the modern Serverless + zero-ETL + streaming options.
questions.pattern: Data-warehouse architecture: Redshift (columnar MPP). Athena (SQL on S3, serverless). EMR (Spark/Hive/Presto for very flexible). Choose: Athena for ad-hoc on data lake, Redshift for BI workloads with predictable users, Lakehouse pattern with Iceberg + Athena/EMR/Redshift Spectrum for unified.

questions.scenario: A company is designing a new system that processes 10M short-lived (50ms) HTTP requests/sec via a Lambda function. They need to manage cold starts, function packaging, and reuse of expensive initialization (database connections).
questions.question: Which Lambda design practices APPLY?
choices: Initialize the database connection inside the handler on every invocation so it's freshly established for each request — fresh connections eliminate stale-connection bugs and Lambda's connection pooling handles the cost
Avoid Lambda for any HTTP work and run on EC2 instead — Lambda is not designed for HTTP request volume and ECS/EKS containers handle high-volume HTTP workloads better than Lambda

Use Provisioned Concurrency (pre-warmed environments, eliminates cold start); initialize expensive resources outside the handler in module init so they reuse across invocations; use SnapStart for Java/Python/.NET for faster startup; tune memory (proportional CPU); use reserved concurrency to cap cost; for HTTP entry — Lambda function URLs, API Gateway, or ALB
Use only the smallest memory configuration (128 MB) for every Lambda — small memory allocations have the fastest cold start and Lambda's CPU is independent of memory configuration
questions.explanation:
Lambda performance and cold-start best practices: (1) **Provisioned Concurrency** — pre-warm N execution environments; eliminates cold-start for predictable traffic; pay per provisioned-environment-second. Combine with autoscaling on Provisioned Concurrency utilization. (2) **Init code outside handler** — execute once per environment lifecycle; reuse DB connection pools, AWS SDK clients, ML model loads. (3) **SnapStart** — Java, Python, .NET — snapshot of initialized state, restored fast on cold start (10x faster). (4) **Memory** — proportional CPU; tune for cost-perf with Lambda Power Tuning. (5) **Container images** — supports up to 10 GB packages; benefit: same artifact for local + Lambda. (6) **VPC** — older 'attach to VPC' had heavy cold-start cost; modern uses Hyperplane ENIs (one ENI per AZ shared across invocations). (7) **HTTP entry** — Lambda function URLs (cheap, simple), ALB (rich routing), API Gateway (auth, throttling). (8) **Reserved concurrency** caps maximum invocations (limits blast radius on bugs / runaway traffic).

Option B causes connection-pool exhaustion. Option C starves CPU. Option D is wrong. The trap: candidates may not realize handler-outside vs handler-inside init has huge perf implications.
questions.pattern: Lambda design checklist: handler thin, init outside; Provisioned Concurrency for cold-start-sensitive; SnapStart for JVM/.NET; right-size memory; reserved concurrency cap; X-Ray tracing; structured logging; idempotent handlers (retries are common); DLQ for failures; Lambda Powertools libraries for observability + helpers.

questions.scenario: A company designs a new system requiring strong workflow orchestration — long-running multi-step processes with retries, parallelism, error handling, human approvals, integration with 200+ AWS services.
questions.question: Which AWS service is PURPOSE-BUILT for orchestration?
choices: AWS Step Functions — serverless workflow service with state machines (Standard for long-running with full audit, Express for high-volume short workflows); supports parallel branches, retry-with-backoff, error handling, choice (branching), wait, and direct AWS service integrations to 220+ services; Workflow Studio for visual authoring
Cron jobs on a dedicated EC2 instance with shell scripts orchestrating workflow steps — this approach offers maximum flexibility and direct integration with any AWS service via the AWS CLI

Manual coordination via shared spreadsheets with team members marking each step complete — the human-in-the-loop pattern is more reliable than automated orchestrators for complex 200-service integrations
Amazon SQS with chained queues, where each step writes to the next queue — the message-passing pattern handles long-running workflows with retries and parallelism via SQS visibility timeouts
questions.explanation:
AWS Step Functions is the canonical orchestrator: (1) **Standard workflow** — up to 1 year duration, full audit history (every state transition logged), $$/transition, ideal for long-running and human-in-loop. (2) **Express workflow** — up to 5 minutes, async or sync (return result), $/million transitions, ideal for high-volume / event-driven / API-backed. (3) **States** — Pass, Task, Choice (branching), Wait (timed), Parallel, Map (iterate over array, parallelism configurable), Succeed, Fail. (4) **Retry / Catch** — declarative retry-with-backoff; error handlers route to compensation steps. (5) **Direct service integrations** — call SDK actions on 220+ services without Lambda glue (DynamoDB, S3, SNS, SQS, ECS, EMR, SageMaker, Glue, etc.). (6) **Workflow Studio** — visual authoring; ASL (Amazon States Language) JSON definition. (7) **Synchronous Express** for API responses; **callback patterns** (waitForTaskToken) for async/human-in-loop. (8) **MWAA** is alternative for Python/data engineering teams familiar with Airflow.

Options B, C are anti-patterns. Option D is data store, not orchestrator. The trap: candidates may have heard 'Step Functions' but not appreciate the depth.
questions.pattern: Orchestration ladder: Step Functions (general orchestration, both human and machine workflows). MWAA (Airflow, Python-DAG-friendly, big-data ecosystem). EventBridge Pipes (transform-and-route between sources/targets). SageMaker Pipelines (ML-specific). Glue Workflows (ETL job orchestration). Choose by team familiarity, workflow complexity, and ML vs general.

questions.scenario: A company designs a new mobile app with millions of users. Backend needs: per-user data, social-graph queries, real-time updates pushed to clients, GraphQL API.
questions.question: Which AWS architecture FITS?
choices: Amazon S3 with hosted JSON files served via CloudFront — static JSON delivered from edge locations eliminates the need for a backend and scales to millions of users at minimal cost
Build the entire backend on raw EC2 with custom Node.js servers and PostgreSQL — full control over the stack offers the best mobile-app performance and avoids AWS managed-service limits

AWS AppSync — managed GraphQL service with real-time subscriptions over WebSockets, integrations with DynamoDB / Lambda / OpenSearch / RDS / HTTP, conflict resolution for offline sync; for social graph, Amazon Neptune (managed graph database with Gremlin / openCypher / SPARQL); Cognito for identity; DynamoDB for per-user data; Pinpoint for engagement
Use API Gateway REST APIs with DynamoDB direct integration — REST handles the per-user data and real-time updates via Server-Sent Events without requiring GraphQL or AppSync
questions.explanation:
Mobile/web backend stack on AWS: (1) **AppSync** — managed GraphQL with real-time subscriptions (WebSockets), offline sync (DataStore client SDKs), pluggable resolvers (DynamoDB direct, Lambda, OpenSearch, RDS, HTTP), Pipeline Resolvers for complex flows, fine-grained auth (Cognito User Pools, IAM, API Keys, OIDC, Lambda). (2) **Neptune** — managed graph database for social, fraud, recommendations; supports Gremlin, openCypher, SPARQL; serverless option available. (3) **Cognito** — User Pools (identity provider with sign-up/sign-in, MFA, federated identity), Identity Pools (temp AWS creds for authenticated app users). (4) **DynamoDB** — per-user data (single-digit ms), Global Tables for multi-region. (5) **Pinpoint** — push notifications, email, SMS, segmentation. (6) **S3 + CloudFront** for assets. (7) **Amplify** — high-level framework that wires many of these together for mobile/web devs.

Options B, C, D are inadequate. The trap: candidates may not know AppSync (vs alternatives like API Gateway WebSockets or Apollo on Lambda).
questions.pattern: Mobile backend stack: AppSync (GraphQL) or API Gateway (REST/WebSocket). Cognito (auth). DynamoDB (data). Lambda (logic). Pinpoint (engagement). S3 + CloudFront (assets). Amplify (developer experience). For social/recommendations: Neptune (graph) + Personalize (ML). For search: OpenSearch. For media: MediaConvert + IVS.

questions.scenario: A company designs a new file-processing pipeline: users upload files via web, files trigger workflows (virus scan, transcoding, indexing), processed files stored, completed notification.
questions.question: Which event-driven architecture is APPROPRIATE?
choices: FTP server on EC2 receiving uploads with a cron job triggering processing every 5 minutes — FTP has lower overhead than presigned S3 URLs and the cron-based polling handles the workflow trigger
Synchronous upload-then-process via single API Gateway call that holds the connection until processing completes — modern API Gateway timeouts allow long-running processing without async architecture

Web client uploads to S3 via presigned POST/PUT URL; S3 event triggers EventBridge or S3 Event Notifications; routed to Step Functions for orchestration (scan + transcode + index in parallel) or Lambda for simpler flows; Step Functions invokes ECS/Fargate for heavy processing (transcoding) and Lambda for light steps; SNS or SES for notifications

Email files as attachments to a workflow address that processes them server-side — email-based workflows offer the simplest user experience without requiring presigned URLs or event-driven architecture
questions.explanation:
Event-driven file processing pattern: (1) **Upload** — pre-signed S3 PUT/POST URL from backend; client uploads directly to S3 (no proxy bottleneck). (2) **Trigger** — S3 → EventBridge (rich filtering) or S3 Event Notifications (simpler). (3) **Orchestration** — Step Functions for multi-step (scan → if clean: transcode + index in parallel → consolidate → notify); Lambda for single-step. (4) **Heavy processing** — ECS/Fargate or Batch for CPU/GPU/memory-intensive (video transcoding); Lambda for light (metadata, thumbnails). (5) **Storage** — processed files to S3, optionally with Intelligent Tiering. (6) **Notifications** — SNS topic → email/SMS/Lambda; SES for transactional email; Pinpoint for app push. (7) **On-the-fly transforms** — S3 Object Lambda transforms responses (e.g., redact PII, resize images) without storing variants. (8) **Async to user** — API Gateway returns 202 Accepted with status URL; client polls or subscribes via WebSocket.

Option B blocks user during long processing. Options C, D are wrong patterns. The trap: candidates may write synchronous flows when async is the correct architecture.
questions.pattern: Event-driven pipeline: ingest (S3 presigned upload). Trigger (S3 → EventBridge). Orchestrate (Step Functions). Compute (Lambda short, Fargate long, Batch huge). Store (S3 with lifecycle). Notify (SNS, SES, push). Status (DynamoDB + WebSocket / polling endpoint). Idempotent steps for safe retry.

questions.scenario: A company is designing a new Internet-facing application. They need DDoS protection, OWASP-style attack mitigation, bot management, and rate limiting per user.
questions.question: Which AWS edge security stack is APPROPRIATE?
choices: CloudFront in front (TLS termination, edge caching, DDoS absorption); AWS Shield Standard (always on for L3/4) plus Shield Advanced (subscription, expanded protections, 24/7 SRT, cost protection); AWS WAF (managed and custom rules: OWASP Top 10, SQL injection, XSS; rate-based rules per IP/user; CAPTCHA for bots); WAF Bot Control; integrated with ALB/API Gateway/AppSync/Cognito
Rely only on EC2 security groups configured to allow only known good IPs — security groups provide the L7 attack mitigation and bot management that WAF would otherwise handle

Expose the origin servers directly to the internet with no CDN or WAF layer — modern application architectures rely on application-level security alone and DDoS protection is unnecessary at the network edge
Deploy Network Load Balancer with TLS termination and source-IP filtering — NLB's source-IP and rate-limiting features cover OWASP Top 10 mitigation at the network layer
questions.explanation:
Layered edge protection: (1) **CloudFront** — global edge points absorb large-volume DDoS; TLS termination; reduces origin load. (2) **AWS Shield Standard** — free, automatic, mitigates L3/L4 DDoS for CloudFront / Route 53. (3) **Shield Advanced** — paid (~$3K/month); expanded mitigations, 24/7 access to AWS Shield Response Team (SRT), cost protection (refund for DDoS-induced scaling), additional metrics, integration with Route 53/ALB/etc. (4) **AWS WAF** — Layer 7: managed rule groups (OWASP, AWS-managed core, IP reputation lists), custom rules, rate-based rules (cap requests per IP per 5 min), regex matching, geo blocking, CAPTCHA. (5) **WAF Bot Control** — managed rule group identifies bot traffic, allows legitimate (search engines), blocks/challenges others. (6) **AWS Firewall Manager** — central management of WAF/Shield/Network Firewall across accounts. (7) **Origin protection** — restrict origin to accept only from CloudFront via Origin Access Control + secret-header validation.

Option B exposes everything. Options C, D are inadequate. The trap: candidates may know WAF but not the full stack.
questions.pattern: Edge security stack: CloudFront + Shield Standard (default). + Shield Advanced (high-value targets). + WAF (custom + managed rules). + WAF Bot Control. + Firewall Manager (centralize). Origin restriction. Geo-restrictions per use case. Monitor with CloudWatch + AWS Network Firewall for VPC-egress / east-west.

questions.scenario: A company designs a new cost-optimized batch processing system. Workloads run nightly, are interruptible, processing time varies. They want to minimize cost.
questions.question: Which AWS compute approach is OPTIMAL for batch + interruptible?
choices: Run the batch workload on On-Demand EC2 instances 24x7 — On-Demand pricing offers reserved capacity reliability and the cost premium is justified by the predictable runtime
Run all batch workloads on a single t3.micro instance to minimize cost — small instances stretch the workload over more hours but the per-hour rate is the lowest of any instance class

AWS Batch on EC2 Spot Instances or Fargate Spot for serverless containers; use multiple instance types in compute environments plus capacity-optimized allocation strategy (lowest interruption); for ad-hoc data engineering, EMR on Spot; up to 90% discount; combine with Savings Plans for predictable baseline; AWS Compute Optimizer to right-size
Use Lambda with the maximum 15-minute timeout configured per invocation — Lambda's per-invocation pricing is the most cost-effective compute pattern for any batch workload
questions.explanation:
Cost-optimized batch architectures: (1) **AWS Batch** — managed batch with array jobs, dependencies, job queues; runs on EC2 (with Spot) or Fargate (with Fargate Spot); for HPC-style: EC2 with capacity-optimized Spot allocation. (2) **Spot Instances** — up to 90% off; interruptible (2-min warning); best practices: use multiple instance types/families/AZs (interruption rate is per-pool), capacity-optimized allocation strategy (chooses pools with most spare capacity). (3) **Spot Fleet / EC2 Fleet / Auto Scaling Group with mixed instances** — diversification. (4) **EMR Spot** — for Spark/Hadoop batch with checkpoint-friendly jobs. (5) **SageMaker Managed Spot Training** — automatic for ML training jobs. (6) **Savings Plans** for predictable baseline (steady-state) — Compute Savings Plans most flexible (any region, any instance family). (7) **Reserved Instances** for predictable RDS/Redshift. (8) **Compute Optimizer** recommends right-sizing.

Option B is most expensive. Option C bottlenecks throughput. Option D doesn't fit Lambda's 15-min timeout. The trap: candidates may not know capacity-optimized Spot strategies that minimize interruption.
questions.pattern: Cost discount mechanisms: Spot (variable, deepest discount, interruptible). Savings Plans (committed compute hours, ~30-72% off). Reserved Instances (RDS/Redshift/specific instances). Right-sizing (Compute Optimizer). Auto-scaling. Schedule-based start/stop (dev/test). Spot best practices: capacity-optimized, instance diversification, checkpoint frequently.

questions.scenario: A company is designing a new system requiring secure secrets management: DB credentials, API keys, third-party tokens. Secrets must be rotated automatically, audited, encrypted, and accessible from EC2/Lambda/ECS without storing in environment variables.
questions.question: Which AWS service combination fits?
choices: AWS Secrets Manager — automated rotation built-in for RDS/Aurora/Redshift/DocumentDB plus custom Lambda for others, KMS encryption, IAM access control, CloudTrail audit, replication across regions; integrated with ECS task definitions, Lambda Parameters and Secrets extension, and Kubernetes via CSI driver / external-secrets; Parameter Store for non-rotating config
Store secrets as plaintext environment variables visible in the AWS Console — environment variables are the AWS-recommended pattern because they propagate through all compute services automatically
Hardcode secrets in source code committed to a private CodeCommit repository — private repositories provide adequate access control and version-controlled secrets are easily auditable
Email passwords to each application owner with quarterly rotation reminders — manual rotation by application owners provides better accountability than automated rotation services
questions.explanation:
AWS Secrets Manager: (1) **Storage** — encrypted with KMS (customer or AWS-managed key); IAM-controlled access. (2) **Rotation** — built-in for RDS/Aurora/DocumentDB/Redshift; Lambda-based custom rotation for any secret; configure rotation schedule. (3) **Cross-region replication** — Secrets Manager replicates secrets to other regions. (4) **Audit** — CloudTrail logs every GetSecretValue with caller identity. (5) **Integration patterns** — ECS task definitions reference secrets directly (injected as env vars); Lambda Parameters and Secrets Extension caches secrets locally with TTL (avoids hitting API on every invocation); Kubernetes Secrets Store CSI Driver / external-secrets-operator. (6) **Parameter Store** (Systems Manager) — cheaper alternative for non-rotating config and non-sensitive parameters; SecureString parameters for secrets that don't need rotation. (7) **Cost-conscious** combination: Parameter Store for static secrets, Secrets Manager for rotating.

Options B, C, D are anti-patterns. The trap: candidates may use Parameter Store for everything (cheaper) but miss Secrets Manager's rotation capability.
questions.pattern: Secrets architecture: Secrets Manager (rotating, expensive). Parameter Store SecureString (cheap, static). KMS for encryption. IAM for access. CloudTrail for audit. Application access: ECS secrets, Lambda extension, K8s CSI. Avoid: plaintext env vars, hardcoded, public repos. For client-side encryption beyond at-rest: AWS Encryption SDK or KMS GenerateDataKey.

questions.scenario: A team's existing application uses RDS PostgreSQL (single AZ) at 80% CPU during peak. Storage is filling. The DB is a bottleneck.
questions.question: Which IMPROVEMENTS apply for an EXISTING workload's database?
choices: Disable backups to free up storage and CPU — RDS backups consume the resources needed for peak load, and modern PITR is unnecessary for production databases with read replicas

Enable Multi-AZ for HA, right-size the instance, add Read Replicas to offload reads, use Performance Insights and Enhanced Monitoring to find slow queries (index and optimize), consider Aurora migration for cloud-native scaling, add ElastiCache for read-heavy caching, enable storage autoscaling, and consider RDS Proxy for connection storms
Ignore the issue — RDS automatically handles peak load through transparent under-the-hood scaling and the 80% CPU at peak is well within normal operating range for production databases
Switch to DynamoDB regardless of relational requirements — DynamoDB's NoSQL scalability automatically handles relational workloads when secondary indexes are configured to mimic foreign keys
questions.explanation:
RDS optimization ladder: (1) **Multi-AZ** — synchronous standby; HA + automatic failover; no app code change; not for read scaling. (2) **Scale-up** — bigger instance class; vertical scaling. (3) **Read Replicas** — async replicas; offload read queries; up to 15 (5 for traditional MySQL/Postgres; 15 for Aurora). (4) **Diagnose slow queries** — Performance Insights (visualizes wait events), Enhanced Monitoring (host metrics), explain plans. Index, query optimization, schema changes can be 10-100x improvements. (5) **Aurora migration** — cloud-native, distributed storage (auto-scales), faster failover (~30s), read replicas with ms lag. Migration via DMS or snapshot. (6) **Caching** — ElastiCache (Redis or Memcached) in front of DB for read-heavy patterns; reduce DB load. (7) **Storage autoscaling** — RDS automatically grows storage; enable to avoid manual intervention. (8) **Connection pooling** — RDS Proxy reduces connection overhead.

Option B is anti-pattern. Option C abandons relational requirements. Option D is reckless. The trap: candidates may default to 'switch to Aurora' without first checking whether index/query optimization solves it.
questions.pattern: DB performance investigation: query analysis (Performance Insights, EXPLAIN). Index optimization. Schema changes. Vertical scaling. Read replicas + caching. Architecture (Aurora migration). RDS Proxy for connection storms. For very high write throughput, consider sharding or DynamoDB (if relational not strictly needed).

questions.scenario: A team's existing 3-tier web application on EC2 + RDS sees periodic outages. Investigation reveals a single web tier instance, single AZ, no auto-recovery. They want to improve resilience without rewriting code.
questions.question: Which AWS-native changes IMPROVE RESILIENCE quickly?
choices: Email users a status update during the periodic outages — proactive communication offsets the availability impact and is more cost-effective than the engineering effort to fix the architecture
Use a larger instance class in a single AZ — modern AWS instances have 99.99% AZ-level SLAs that match multi-AZ availability without the complexity of cross-AZ deployment
Run more processes on the existing single instance to handle the load — vertical scaling within a single instance offers the highest performance and avoids the complexity of distributed systems

Auto Scaling Group across ≥2 AZs with min/max/desired (≥2 healthy at all times); Application Load Balancer in front (cross-zone load balancing, health checks, automatic traffic shifting away from unhealthy instances); RDS Multi-AZ; ElastiCache for session state so user sessions survive instance loss; launch templates with the latest AMI for quick replacement
questions.explanation:
Quick resilience improvements without code change: (1) **Auto Scaling Group across ≥2 AZs** — distributes instances across AZs; ASG replaces unhealthy/terminated instances automatically; set MinSize ≥ 2 (so loss of 1 doesn't kill). (2) **ALB** — health-check-driven traffic shifting; cross-zone load balancing; HTTPS termination. ALB itself is multi-AZ. (3) **RDS Multi-AZ** — synchronous standby; automatic failover (~60-120s for traditional RDS, faster for Aurora). (4) **Stateless web tier** — store session state externally (ElastiCache Redis cluster, DynamoDB, or sticky sessions if app is stateful — least preferred). (5) **Launch templates** — versioned, with latest AMI; ASG launches instances from template. (6) **Health checks** — ELB health checks (app-level), EC2 status checks, custom CloudWatch alarms triggering replacement. (7) **CloudWatch + EventBridge** — alert + automate. (8) **For larger upgrade**: containerize → ECS/Fargate or EKS — even faster recovery.

Option B doesn't fix single-AZ failure. Option C still single-AZ. Option D doesn't help. The trap: candidates may know auto-scaling but not the multi-AZ + LB + stateless triad.
questions.pattern: Resilience tier 1 (within region): multi-AZ deployment, auto-scaling, load balancer, externalized state, automatic replacement. Tier 2: multi-region (active-passive or active-active with Route 53 health checks). Tier 3: account-level isolation. Use Resilience Hub to score and recommend.

questions.scenario: A team's existing app stores 500 TB of data in S3 Standard storage class. Most data is rarely accessed after 90 days but occasionally retrieved.
questions.question: Which S3 storage class strategy OPTIMIZES cost?
choices: Keep everything in S3 Standard storage class forever — modern S3 Standard pricing is competitive with archival tiers when total volume exceeds 100 TB, removing the need for tiering
