S3 Lifecycle policies transitioning data to S3 Standard-IA after 30-90 days, then to Glacier Instant Retrieval, then Glacier Flexible Retrieval, then Glacier Deep Archive; OR S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves data between access tiers based on observed access patterns; Storage Lens for visibility; S3 Inventory for full picture
Move all 500 TB to a public S3 bucket and rely on CloudFront caching to absorb the access cost — public buckets have lower per-GB pricing than private buckets when CloudFront fronts the access
Delete all data older than 90 days immediately — the cost of retrieval after deletion is acceptable for rarely-accessed data and lifecycle deletion outperforms cold-storage tiering
questions.explanation:
S3 storage tiering: (1) **Standard** — frequent access, low latency, normal pricing. (2) **Intelligent-Tiering** — auto-moves objects between Frequent/Infrequent/Archive Instant/Archive/Deep Archive based on observed access patterns; small per-object monitoring fee, but eliminates the need to predict access patterns. (3) **Standard-IA** — infrequent access, lower storage cost, retrieval fee per GB; minimum storage 30 days. (4) **Glacier Instant Retrieval** — ms retrieval, lower cost than Standard-IA, min 90 days. (5) **Glacier Flexible Retrieval (formerly 'Glacier')** — minutes (Expedited), hours (Standard), bulk (5-12 hours); much cheaper. (6) **Glacier Deep Archive** — cheapest tier, 12+ hour retrieval (or Bulk 48-hour); compliance archives. (7) **Lifecycle policies** — declarative rules (e.g., 'after 30 days move to IA, after 90 to Glacier'). (8) **Visibility** — S3 Storage Lens shows org-wide usage; S3 Inventory + Athena for queries. For unpredictable access patterns, Intelligent-Tiering is often the simplest win.

Option B wastes money. Option C destroys retrievability. Option D is dangerous. The trap: candidates may not realize Intelligent-Tiering is often the easiest cost win.
questions.pattern: S3 cost optimization: lifecycle to colder tiers (predictable patterns). Intelligent-Tiering (unpredictable). Delete obsolete (lifecycle expirations). Versioning + delete-old-versions lifecycle. Multipart abort cleanup. Compression (Zstd, Snappy for Parquet). Storage Lens + Inventory for visibility. For hot reads on subset: S3 Express One Zone (fastest, single-AZ).

questions.scenario: A team's existing application is mostly EC2-based but has growing operational toil: patching, scaling, managing OS. They want to modernize without a full rewrite.
questions.question: Which incremental MODERNIZATION paths fit?
choices: Migrate everything to a single physical server in a colocation facility — colocated infrastructure offers lower per-month costs than AWS for predictable workloads and reduces the operational toil
Stay on EC2 forever and absorb the operational toil — modernization risks exceed the toil savings for any production workload because of the regression-test burden

Containerize via Docker and run on ECS with Fargate (serverless containers, no EC2 management) or EKS for Kubernetes-shop teams; rehost lift-and-shift via Application Migration Service for VMs that aren't ready to containerize; serverless components for new features (Lambda, Step Functions); managed services for stateful pieces (RDS → Aurora, self-managed Redis → ElastiCache, Kafka → MSK or Kinesis); use AWS Application Composer / SAM / CDK for IaC
Migrate all workloads to managed spreadsheets in Amazon QuickSight — QuickSight handles the data and compute model for any modernized workload at minimal management overhead
questions.explanation:
Modernization patterns (the '7 Rs' for migration extended): (1) **Rehost** — lift-and-shift (Application Migration Service / MGN). Quick, no code change, no immediate cost benefit. (2) **Replatform** — minor changes for cloud benefits (e.g., RDS instead of self-managed). (3) **Refactor** — bigger code changes (containerize, decouple). (4) **Repurchase** — switch to SaaS. (5) **Retire** — kill unused. (6) **Retain** — keep on-prem if cloud doesn't help. (7) **Relocate** — move VMware to VMware Cloud on AWS. **Containerization paths**: ECS (managed, native AWS) on Fargate (serverless) for fewest ops; ECS on EC2 for cost optimization; EKS for Kubernetes ecosystem. **Serverless replacements**: Lambda + Step Functions for new features; managed databases (Aurora, DynamoDB); managed messaging (SQS, SNS, EventBridge, MSK). **IaC**: AWS Application Composer (visual), SAM (Serverless), CDK (programmatic), CloudFormation, Terraform. Modernize incrementally — strangler fig pattern (route traffic gradually to new pieces).

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may push 'rewrite everything' instead of incremental modernization.
questions.pattern: Modernization stages: rehost (MGN). Replatform (managed services). Containerize (ECS/EKS, Fargate for less ops). Refactor (microservices, serverless). Pattern: strangler fig — incrementally route to new components. Tools: MGN, App2Container (Java/.NET), AWS Migration Hub (orchestration), Database Migration Service (DMS) + Schema Conversion Tool (SCT).

questions.scenario: A team's existing CI/CD pipeline is slow (45 min builds), brittle, and has no security scanning. They want to upgrade.
questions.question: Which improvements APPLY?
choices: Skip all testing in the pipeline to reduce the 45-minute build time — production traffic catches the same issues as test suites and the 45-minute saving outweighs the rare bug escape

Parallelize independent build/test steps; cache dependencies (CodeBuild local cache or S3 cache); use larger CodeBuild compute when justified; integrate security scanning (Inspector for ECR images and EC2, CodeGuru Reviewer for code, SAST/DAST tools, secret scanning); IaC scanning (cfn-nag, tfsec, Checkov) in pipeline; require approvals for production via CodePipeline manual approval; track DORA metrics; immutable releases (versioned artifacts)
Manual deployments to production by a single experienced engineer — manual deployments offer better safety than automated CI/CD because the engineer applies judgment to each release
Eliminate CI/CD entirely and rely on developers to test locally before merging to main — local testing catches more issues than automated pipelines because developers know the codebase deeply
questions.explanation:
Modern CI/CD improvements: (1) **Parallelize** — break long pipeline into parallel stages where possible; CodeBuild parallel jobs, CodePipeline parallel actions. (2) **Caching** — CodeBuild local cache (Docker layers, dependencies), S3 cache, ECR layer cache. (3) **Right-size build resources** — bigger CodeBuild instance types for CPU-bound; matrix builds. (4) **Security scanning in pipeline** — Inspector (ECR images, EC2 vulnerabilities), CodeGuru Reviewer (Java/Python static analysis), SAST/DAST tools, secret scanning (git-secrets, truffleHog, GitHub Advanced Security), IaC scanning (cfn-nag, Checkov, tfsec, KICS). (5) **Approvals** — CodePipeline manual approval action for production; SNS notification + signoff. (6) **DORA metrics** — deployment frequency, lead time, MTTR, change failure rate. (7) **Immutable releases** — version every artifact (container images by tag/SHA, Lambda by version, IaC by Git SHA). (8) **Observability** — pipeline metrics, build logs to CloudWatch, anomaly detection.

Options B, C, D are anti-patterns. The trap: candidates may know CI/CD generally but not the specific improvements that drive speed and security simultaneously.
questions.pattern: CI/CD improvements: parallelize, cache, right-size. Test pyramid (unit > integration > e2e). Shift left security (scanning in PR). IaC scanning. Manual approvals for prod. Immutable artifacts. Observability of pipeline. AWS-native: CodeCatalyst (DevOps platform), CodePipeline, CodeBuild, CodeDeploy, CodeArtifact (artifact repo).

questions.scenario: A team's existing application logs to local disk on EC2 instances. Logs are lost when instances terminate. The team needs centralized observability.
questions.question: Which AWS approach is CORRECT?
choices: CloudWatch Agent on instances (forward logs and custom metrics); for K8s, Fluent Bit/Fluentd to CloudWatch Logs; CloudWatch Container Insights (managed for ECS/EKS); X-Ray for distributed tracing; CloudWatch Application Signals for APM (RED metrics auto-instrumented); CloudWatch Logs Insights for ad-hoc query; subscription filters to OpenSearch / Kinesis Firehose / S3 / Lambda for richer analysis
Email log files to the team daily as compressed attachments — email-based log review provides better signal-to-noise than dashboards because humans triage what matters
Print logs only to the local console of each EC2 instance — local console logging is the AWS-recommended pattern because the logs persist on the instance's local SSD
Don't collect logs and rely on tracing alone — modern AWS X-Ray captures every request flow including stack traces and replaces the need for application logs in cloud-native architectures
questions.explanation:
Modern AWS observability stack: (1) **CloudWatch Agent** on EC2 — forwards logs (with structured fields) + custom metrics (procstat, statsd, collectd integration). (2) **Containers** — Fluent Bit (preferred, lightweight) or Fluentd to CloudWatch Logs; CloudWatch Container Insights for managed metrics + dashboards on ECS/EKS. (3) **X-Ray** — distributed tracing; auto-instrument via SDKs or use ADOT (AWS Distro for OpenTelemetry) for OTel-standard. (4) **CloudWatch Application Signals** — modern APM; auto-instruments Java/Python (and growing) for RED (Rate, Errors, Duration) metrics + service map + SLOs without writing instrumentation. (5) **Logs Insights** — SQL-like ad-hoc query on logs. (6) **Subscriptions** — stream logs to Lambda (real-time processing), Kinesis Firehose (S3, OpenSearch), or 3rd party. (7) **Application Insights** — auto-discovers app components and recommends monitoring. (8) **Alarms + EventBridge** — alert + automated response.

Options B, C, D are reckless. The trap: candidates may use CloudWatch Logs but miss the structured/observability stack on top.
questions.pattern: Observability layers: metrics (CloudWatch + Container Insights). Logs (CloudWatch Logs + Insights). Traces (X-Ray). APM (Application Signals). Custom (OTel via ADOT, CloudWatch metrics). Externally: Datadog, Splunk, New Relic via subscription filters / OTel exporters. Define SLOs and alert on burn-rate.

questions.scenario: A team's existing serverless backend has grown organically. Functions duplicate logic, deployments are manual zip uploads, dev/prod use the same account, no IaC.
questions.question: Which IMPROVEMENTS apply for an existing serverless mess?
choices: Disable observability to reduce CloudWatch costs — observability adds bill items but rarely catches issues before customers do, making it a low-ROI investment for serverless apps

IaC via AWS SAM, CDK, or Terraform for declarative deployment; account separation (dev / staging / prod via Organizations); Lambda Layers and shared utility libraries to deduplicate; observability via CloudWatch + X-Ray + Application Signals; CI/CD pipeline (CodePipeline / GitHub Actions) for automated, gated deploys; Lambda Powertools libraries for cross-cutting concerns; structured logging plus correlation IDs; secrets in Secrets Manager not env vars
Continue manual zip uploads via the AWS Console — manual deployments give the most control over what reaches production and prevent the configuration drift that IaC introduces
Consolidate all functions into a single monolithic Lambda function — one large function reduces the deployment complexity and avoids the Layer-management overhead of split functions
questions.explanation:
Serverless modernization: (1) **IaC** — AWS SAM (declarative for serverless), CDK (programmatic, multi-language), Terraform (multi-cloud). Define functions, layers, APIs, events, IAM roles in code; deploy reproducibly. (2) **Multi-account** — Organizations + dedicated dev/staging/prod accounts; promote artifacts across accounts via CI/CD. (3) **Lambda Layers** — shared dependencies and utility libraries; reduce duplication. (4) **Lambda Powertools** — open-source library (Python, Java, TypeScript, .NET) for logging, tracing, metrics, parameter retrieval, idempotency, batch processing — best practices baked in. (5) **CI/CD** — gated promotion (CodePipeline, GitHub Actions, GitLab); automated tests in pipeline; blue/green or canary via CodeDeploy. (6) **Observability** — structured logging (Powertools logger), X-Ray traces with correlation IDs, custom metrics, Application Signals for service-level. (7) **Secrets** — Secrets Manager + Parameters and Secrets Lambda Extension. (8) **Code review** — PR-based, security scanning.

Options B, C, D are anti-patterns. The trap: candidates may know SAM/CDK exist but underrate the toolset (Powertools, multi-account separation).
questions.pattern: Serverless maturity progression: manual deploys → SAM/CDK → multi-account → CI/CD with promotion → comprehensive observability → Lambda Powertools + structured patterns → cost monitoring + provisioned concurrency tuning. Avoid: monolith Lambda, no IaC, prod = dev account.

questions.scenario: A team's existing application has unpredictable traffic spikes that cause occasional 5xx errors. Capacity is fixed-size and can't scale fast enough.
questions.question: Which ARCHITECTURAL CHANGES help?
choices: Set capacity at peak forever — predictable peak capacity eliminates spike-handling complexity and the cost premium is offset by the simplicity of single-tier architecture

Decouple via SQS or Kinesis between web tier and processing tier (web returns 202 + queues work, providing back-pressure protection); add ALB plus Auto Scaling Group with target tracking and predictive scaling; use ElastiCache for hot reads; CloudFront caching for cacheable responses; circuit breakers and exponential backoff in clients; for serverless tier, Lambda with reserved concurrency caps plus provisioned concurrency for predictable hot path; rate-limiting at API Gateway / WAF
Disable retries in the web tier — retries amplify the spike load by repeatedly hitting the back-end and removing them lets the back-end recover faster
Email users to slow down their requests during spikes — user-driven rate limiting respects user experience better than automatic throttling and prevents the architectural complexity of queues
questions.explanation:
Burst-handling architectural changes: (1) **Decouple** — async patterns: web tier accepts requests, queues (SQS) or streams (Kinesis), returns 202 Accepted + status URL. Processing tier consumes at its own pace. Decoupling absorbs spikes. (2) **Auto-scaling** — ASG with target tracking (e.g., 60% CPU, 50% requests-per-instance); predictive scaling for known patterns. (3) **Caching** — CloudFront for cacheable responses (significant offload), ElastiCache for hot data (read-through pattern). (4) **Resilience patterns** — circuit breakers (App Mesh, Istio, code-level), exponential backoff with jitter, request timeouts. (5) **Lambda** — reserved concurrency caps (limit blast radius); provisioned concurrency for predictable warm capacity. (6) **Rate limiting** — at API Gateway (per-key throttling, usage plans), WAF (rate-based rules per IP). (7) **Surge queues** — async processing with retry. (8) **Database protection** — RDS Proxy for connection pooling, prevents connection storms.

Option B is wasteful. Option C makes things worse. Option D is absurd. The trap: candidates may scale up without changing architecture; queue-based decoupling is often the bigger win.
questions.pattern: Spike-handling toolbox: decouple (queue/stream). Scale (target tracking, predictive). Cache (CDN + ElastiCache). Limit (rate limits, throttles). Retry-safe (idempotent + backoff + jitter). Circuit-break (avoid cascade). Reserve (concurrency caps). Test (load tests, chaos engineering). Plan capacity headroom (~30% buffer for autoscale lag).

questions.scenario: A team's existing app architecture uses sticky sessions on ALB to maintain user state. They want to deploy faster, scale easier, and survive instance termination without losing user state.
questions.question: Which CHANGE most fundamentally addresses this?
choices: Disable the load balancer entirely and let DNS round-robin handle traffic distribution — DNS-based routing is more flexible than ALB sticky sessions and removes the affinity dependency
Pin every user to one specific instance permanently using ALB target rules — permanent affinity guarantees session persistence and avoids the complexity of externalized session state

Make the app stateless — externalize session state to ElastiCache (Redis), DynamoDB (with TTL), or signed/encrypted JWT cookies; remove sticky-session reliance; new deploys and instance churn no longer invalidate sessions; auto-scaling becomes effective; combine with shared cache (CloudFront, application cache) and stateless authentication (Cognito, OIDC)
Use longer sticky-session TTLs (24 hours) so user sessions persist across instance churn — extended TTLs allow auto-scaling to operate without affecting users
questions.explanation:
Sticky sessions are an architectural smell — they tie users to specific instances, breaking core cloud benefits (scaling, resilience, deploys). Solution: make the app stateless. (1) **Externalize session state** — ElastiCache for Redis (fast, in-memory, pub/sub if needed) is the most common; DynamoDB (with TTL for auto-cleanup); JWT cookies for stateless auth (signed/encrypted on server). (2) **JWT pattern** — server signs token containing user identity + claims; client returns it on each request; server verifies signature; no server-side session lookup needed (or minimal — just blacklist for revoked tokens). (3) **Cognito** for managed user pools and JWT issuance. (4) **Shared cache** — CloudFront caches anonymous responses; application cache for user-specific responses with appropriate keying. (5) **Benefits unlocked**: auto-scaling effective, blue/green deploys without session loss, multi-AZ resilience without affinity, simpler ops. Refactoring to stateless is one of the highest-ROI architectural changes for legacy apps.

Option B doesn't fix the underlying issue. Options C, D are worse. The trap: candidates may layer scaling fixes without addressing the stateful root cause.
questions.pattern: Stateless app benefits: scale up/down freely, deploy without disrupting users, survive instance loss, simpler ops. Where to put state: ElastiCache (sessions, hot data), DynamoDB (durable state, single-digit ms), RDS/Aurora (transactional), JWT (auth state), S3 (large objects). For real-time presence: ElastiCache Redis pub/sub or AppSync subscriptions.

questions.scenario: A team's existing monolith is hard to deploy: every change requires full redeploy + 30-min downtime. They want zero-downtime deploys without rewriting to microservices.
questions.question: Which deployment STRATEGIES enable zero-downtime EVEN FOR MONOLITHS?
choices: Limit deployments to one major release per quarter — slower release cycles reduce the deployment-failure risk and eliminate the need for zero-downtime patterns
Deploy directly to production without testing in any staging environment — modern monoliths are robust enough that production traffic catches any bugs faster than staging does

Blue/green deployment via CodeDeploy (or ECS blue/green if containerized) — provision parallel new environment, smoke-test, swap traffic atomically with auto-rollback on alarm; rolling deployment behind ALB (terminate-and-replace one instance at a time); canary releases (small % first, validate, scale up); database migrations: backward-compatible schema changes (expand-contract pattern), feature flags to decouple deploy from release
Continue 30-minute scheduled downtime during off-peak hours — scheduled maintenance is the AWS-recommended pattern because it provides predictability for users and ops teams
questions.explanation:
Zero-downtime deployment for monoliths: (1) **Blue/green** — CodeDeploy supports for EC2/Lambda/ECS; provision parallel environment, deploy new version there, smoke-test, swap traffic at LB level (or ALB target group swap), auto-rollback on alarm. Old environment kept briefly for fast rollback. (2) **Rolling** — replace instances one at a time behind ALB; ALB drains existing connections, routes new traffic to fresh instances. Slower but lower resource cost. (3) **Canary** — route small % to new (1%, 10%), monitor metrics, scale up if good. (4) **Backward-compatible DB migrations** — expand-contract pattern: deploy schema change first (compatible with both old + new code), then deploy code, then clean up old schema. Avoid breaking changes. (5) **Feature flags** — deploy code with feature off, enable later via config (LaunchDarkly, AppConfig, custom); decouples deploy from release. (6) **App Runner / Elastic Beanstalk** auto-handle blue/green, rolling. (7) **Health checks gate traffic** — ALB only routes to healthy targets.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may think zero-downtime requires microservices.
questions.pattern: Zero-downtime deploy: deployment pattern (blue/green, rolling, canary). Backward-compatible schema. Feature flags. Health checks. Auto-rollback. Test in staging. Automate via CodeDeploy / CodePipeline. Validate via canary metrics. CI/CD with promotion gates.

questions.scenario: A team's existing 100-account organization has 'shadow IT' problems — teams spin up unapproved services or expensive resources, leading to surprise bills.
questions.question: Which controls REDUCE shadow-IT risk while enabling team velocity?
choices: Manually review every API call before it executes — pre-flight review catches every shadow-IT attempt and is the only reliable preventive control for unapproved services

Trust everyone with full AdministratorAccess and rely on culture for cost discipline — high-trust environments outperform locked-down environments because developers self-correct when given visibility

SCPs (deny risky/expensive services or restrict to specific accounts); AWS Service Catalog (curated approved infrastructure templates teams self-service deploy with parameter constraints); AWS Budgets plus Cost Anomaly Detection alerts; Compute Optimizer recommendations; tagging policies for cost allocation and accountability; AWS Control Tower with required guardrails; permission boundaries on developer roles; FinOps practice plus showback dashboards
Block all AWS access except through a single approval queue managed by a centralized cloud team — centralization eliminates shadow IT and is the AWS-recommended governance model for large orgs
questions.explanation:
Shadow-IT mitigation balancing governance and velocity: (1) **SCPs** — deny expensive or risky services (e.g., specific GPU instance types, SageMaker training in non-data-science accounts); deny actions in unapproved regions. (2) **Service Catalog** — curated 'menu' of approved infra templates (CloudFormation/Terraform); teams self-service within constraints; products versioned, parameter-validated. Reduces 'reinvent the wheel' and ensures compliance. (3) **AWS Budgets** — set per-team or per-account budgets with alerts. (4) **Cost Anomaly Detection** — ML-based, surfaces unusual spend. (5) **Compute Optimizer** — surfaces over-provisioning. (6) **Tagging Policies** — declarative tag standards (`CostCenter`, `Project`, `Owner`); enforce via SCPs (deny RunInstances without required tags). (7) **Showback / FinOps** — Cost and Usage Report → Athena → QuickSight per-team dashboards; team-level accountability changes behavior. (8) **Control Tower guardrails** — preventive + detective.

Options B, C, D are extremes. The trap: candidates may pick all-or-nothing (full lockdown vs free-for-all); the answer is curated empowerment.
questions.pattern: Shadow-IT controls: prevent (SCPs, RCPs, permission boundaries). Enable (Service Catalog, Cloud Formation/CDK templates, paved-road platforms). Detect (Config rules, Trusted Advisor, Cost Anomaly). Account (tagging + showback). Educate (FinOps + cloud center of excellence). Bias toward enablement-with-guardrails over lockdown.

questions.scenario: A team's existing analytics workload runs on EMR Hadoop. Job startup is slow (30-min cluster spin-up), the cluster sits idle between jobs, and Hive queries are sometimes cancelled.
questions.question: Which IMPROVEMENTS apply for batch analytics modernization?
choices: Run all analytics queries directly on Amazon RDS — RDS handles 1 PB workloads at the required latency and removes the need for a separate analytics platform

EMR transient clusters (spin up per job, terminate after); EMR Serverless (no cluster management, auto-scale); EMR on EKS (run Spark on shared K8s); migrate to Athena (serverless SQL on S3) for ad-hoc queries; Glue ETL (serverless Spark) for ETL; modernize storage to Iceberg/Delta/Hudi for ACID, time travel, schema evolution; Spot for cost
Keep the EMR cluster running 24x7 idle — keeping the cluster warm avoids the 30-minute startup time and the idle cost is offset by the productivity gain
Disable analytics workloads entirely — modern data-driven products do not need separate analytics workloads because operational dashboards capture the same insights
questions.explanation:
Modernize batch analytics: (1) **EMR transient clusters** — spin up per job via Step Functions / managed scaling; terminate after; pay only for use. (2) **EMR Serverless** — managed, no cluster lifecycle; auto-scales workers per job; per-second billing; ideal for variable workloads. (3) **EMR on EKS** — share K8s cluster across Spark jobs and other workloads; better resource utilization. (4) **Athena** — serverless SQL on S3 (Trino under the hood); pay per TB scanned; ideal for ad-hoc queries; partition projection for performance; Athena Federated Query into other sources. (5) **Glue ETL** — serverless Spark; visual or code; integrates with Glue Data Catalog. (6) **Modern table formats** — Apache Iceberg / Delta Lake / Hudi for ACID transactions, schema evolution, time travel; supported by Athena, EMR, Spark, Glue. (7) **Spot Instances** — for interruption-tolerant Spark jobs; up to 90% off. (8) **Glue Data Catalog** + **Lake Formation** for unified metadata + governance across engines.

Option B wastes money. Options C, D are wrong fits. The trap: candidates may know EMR but not the serverless options that eliminate the lifecycle problem.
questions.pattern: Analytics modernization: EMR persistent → EMR transient → EMR Serverless or Athena. Hive → Spark/Iceberg. Self-managed → managed. Always-on → on-demand. Track-table-format: Parquet (basic) → Iceberg/Delta/Hudi (ACID + evolution). Catalog: Hive → Glue Data Catalog. Governance: Lake Formation.

questions.scenario: A company is migrating 500 on-prem VMs to AWS. They want minimum disruption: continuous replication of VMs, cutover at chosen time, ability to test in cloud before cutover.
questions.question: Which AWS service is PURPOSE-BUILT for VM migration?
choices: Email VM disk images one at a time to AWS via SES — email transport handles disk images of any size and is the simplest migration path for organizations without DX or VPN

AWS Application Migration Service (AWS MGN) — agent-based continuous block-level replication of source VMs (physical or virtual, any hypervisor) to AWS; launch test instances in AWS without affecting source; cutover when ready (downtime in minutes); supports Windows and Linux; alternative: AWS DataSync for bulk file/object data, AWS DMS for databases (often paired with MGN for app+DB migration)
Use only S3 to store VM disk snapshots — S3 is the canonical migration target because it integrates with EC2's launch-from-S3 feature for instant VM provisioning
Manually rebuild every VM in AWS by reading the on-prem documentation — manual rebuilds catch configuration drift and produce a clean state in AWS that automated migration cannot
questions.explanation:
AWS Application Migration Service (MGN) is the rehost lift-and-shift service: (1) **Agent-based replication** — install agent on source VM (Windows / Linux); MGN continuously replicates block-level changes to AWS (Replication Server in AWS receives). (2) **Initial sync** — completes over time (depends on bandwidth + data size). (3) **Test in cloud** — launch test instances from current replicated state without disrupting source; validate apps work. (4) **Cutover** — at chosen time, finalize replication, launch production instances, redirect traffic. Downtime ~ minutes. (5) **Post-cutover** — decommission source after validation. (6) **Hypervisor agnostic** — VMware, Hyper-V, KVM, physical. (7) **Migration Hub** — orchestrate / track waves of migration. (8) **DataSync** — for bulk file/object transfer (S3, FSx, EFS). (9) **DMS** — Database Migration Service for relational DB migration (often homogeneous like Oracle→Oracle or heterogeneous like Oracle→PostgreSQL with Schema Conversion Tool).

Option B is absurd. Option C is high-risk + slow. Option D doesn't migrate apps. The trap: candidates may know S3 but not the migration-specific services.
questions.pattern: Migration toolkit: MGN (VMs, lift-and-shift). DMS + SCT (databases). DataSync (files, objects). Snowball Edge (offline bulk transfer for poor connectivity). Migration Hub (orchestration, waves). App2Container (Java/.NET to containers). Migration Acceleration Program (MAP) for funded migrations.

questions.scenario: A company is migrating 100 TB of file data from on-prem NFS to AWS. They want bandwidth-efficient incremental sync, with scheduling and verification.
questions.question: Which AWS service is APPROPRIATE?
choices: Email files one by one as compressed attachments — email transports any file size and avoids the agent-installation complexity of DataSync
Manual SCP via a bastion host for each file — SCP gives the most control over the transfer and the verification handshake is more reliable than DataSync's automated checksums

AWS DataSync — agent in source environment, data transfer to S3/EFS/FSx; bandwidth throttling; scheduled or on-demand transfers; incremental syncs (only changed files); verification via checksums; supports NFS, SMB, HDFS, S3, Azure Files, Google Cloud Storage; for offline very-large transfers (PBs over slow links): AWS Snowball Edge
Wait years for slow upload via the AWS Console drag-and-drop interface — Console upload is the AWS-recommended path for any file size and the time-to-completion is acceptable for one-time migrations
questions.explanation:
AWS DataSync is the managed file/object transfer service: (1) **Agent** — deployed in source environment (VM, container, EC2). (2) **Sources/destinations** — NFS, SMB, HDFS (Hadoop), self-managed object storage, S3, EFS, FSx (Windows, Lustre, OpenZFS, NetApp ONTAP), Azure Files / Blob, Google Cloud Storage. (3) **Bandwidth control** — configurable throttle. (4) **Incremental** — efficient delta transfers (only changed files). (5) **Verification** — checksum-based; ensures data integrity. (6) **Scheduling** — time-based or on-demand via API. (7) **Filter rules** — include/exclude patterns. (8) **For PB-scale or poor connectivity** — AWS Snow Family (Snowball Edge for ~80 TB devices, multiple in parallel; Snowmobile is being retired). (9) **Direct Connect / VPN endpoints** for private transfer paths. (10) **Storage Gateway** for hybrid file/cache access (different use case — gradual migration with on-prem cache).

Options B, C, D are absurd. The trap: candidates may default to S3 CLI sync, missing DataSync's verification, throttling, and orchestration.
questions.pattern: Bulk data movement: DataSync (online, agent-based). Snowball Edge (offline, devices shipped). Storage Gateway (hybrid file/cache). DMS (databases). Direct Connect (network capacity for ongoing). Choose by data volume, available bandwidth, urgency, recurring vs one-time.

questions.scenario: A company is migrating Oracle DB to PostgreSQL on Aurora. Schema and stored procedures must be converted; data must replicate continuously; cutover with minimal downtime.
questions.question: Which AWS services FIT this MIGRATION?
choices: AWS Schema Conversion Tool (SCT) for schema and stored-procedure conversion (with manual fixes for incompatibilities); AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) with continuous CDC replication — full load plus ongoing changes; cutover when in-sync; for Oracle-specific functions, AWS Application Migration Service or refactor; validate with DMS data validation; migration assessment via SCT
Run two databases (Oracle and Aurora) forever in parallel with application-level dual-writes — dual-writes provide the safest migration path and eliminate the need for cutover
Email the database backup files to AWS support for them to restore into Aurora — AWS Professional Services performs all heterogeneous DB migrations as a managed service
Manual export and import via SQL dumps with a maintenance window of several hours — large databases use this approach because automated migration tools cannot handle Oracle-specific features reliably
questions.explanation:
Heterogeneous DB migration (Oracle → PostgreSQL): (1) **AWS SCT** — analyzes source schema/code; converts to target dialect (Oracle PL/SQL → PostgreSQL plpgsql); produces assessment report listing manual changes needed (often complex stored procedures, Oracle-specific features like sequences, hierarchical queries CONNECT BY, etc.). (2) **DMS** — continuous Change Data Capture (CDC) replication: full initial load + ongoing changes; replicate from on-prem Oracle to Aurora PostgreSQL; multiple replication tasks for parallelism. (3) **Validation** — DMS data validation feature compares source vs target row-by-row. (4) **Cutover** — when DMS is caught up (CDC near-zero lag), pause source writes (or switch them to target), promote target. Downtime: minutes. (5) **Code refactoring** — application code may need changes for SQL dialect differences. (6) **Babelfish for Aurora PostgreSQL** — runs T-SQL (SQL Server dialect) on Aurora — different scenario. (7) **Migration Hub** to track waves.

Option B has long downtime. Options C, D are absurd. The trap: candidates may know DMS but miss SCT's necessity for heterogeneous schema conversion.
questions.pattern: DB migration types: homogeneous (Oracle→Oracle on RDS, MySQL→Aurora MySQL) — DMS only typically. Heterogeneous (Oracle→PostgreSQL, SQL Server→Aurora) — SCT + DMS. Always: assess schema gaps, plan code changes, test, migrate with CDC, cut over with minimal downtime, validate, decommission source.

questions.scenario: A company has a Java EE / .NET monolith on-prem. They want to containerize without manually writing Dockerfiles for every app.
questions.question: Which AWS service ASSISTS containerization of legacy apps?
choices: AWS App2Container (A2C) — analyzes running Java (Tomcat, JBoss) and .NET (IIS) apps on Linux/Windows hosts; generates Dockerfiles, container images, ECS/EKS task definitions / pod specs, and CodePipeline templates; pushes to ECR; reduces effort to containerize legacy workloads; for serverless replacement, refactor identified pieces
Skip containers and refactor everything to AWS Lambda — Lambda's 15-minute limit handles any monolith workload and the per-invocation pricing is more cost-effective than container hosting
Don't containerize and continue running on EC2 — containers add operational complexity that exceeds the agility benefit for legacy applications
Manually rewrite every line of the Java EE / .NET monolith in Go — modern languages produce smaller binaries that fit in containers more efficiently than the legacy stack
questions.explanation:
AWS App2Container (A2C) is the containerization assistant: (1) **Discovery** — scans host for running Java apps (Tomcat, JBoss, WebLogic, etc.) and .NET apps (IIS); identifies dependencies, configs, runtime. (2) **Analysis** — generates artifact inventory + dependencies. (3) **Generation** — produces Dockerfile, container image (pushed to ECR), ECS task definition or EKS manifest, optional CodePipeline template. (4) **Output** — runnable on ECS Fargate / EKS. (5) **Manual cleanup** — generated artifacts often need tweaking (config externalization, logging, secrets); A2C reduces 80% of effort but doesn't replace architecture review. (6) **Combine with App Migration Service (MGN)** for VMs that can't be containerized yet. (7) **For .NET specifically** — Porting Assistant for .NET (analyze code for compatibility with .NET Core/5+/6+/8+ on Linux containers).

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may know to 'containerize' but underrate the toolchain that makes legacy apps containerizable.
questions.pattern: Legacy modernization tools: App2Container (Java/.NET to containers). Porting Assistant for .NET (.NET Framework → .NET Core/8+). Application Migration Service (rehost VMs). DMS + SCT (DBs). Re-platform to managed services (RDS, ElastiCache, Secrets Manager). Refactor incrementally with strangler-fig pattern.

questions.scenario: A company has 200 on-prem applications. Some can move to cloud easily, some require significant effort, some shouldn't move. They need a structured migration approach.
questions.question: Which migration FRAMEWORK guides decisions?
choices: Migrate applications in random order based on developer preference — random ordering produces faster results because parallel teams work without dependencies

Refactor every application to microservices and serverless before migrating — comprehensive refactoring during migration captures the maximum cloud value and is the AWS-recommended approach for any migration

AWS Migration Acceleration Program (MAP) plus the 7 Rs (Rehost, Replatform, Refactor, Repurchase, Retain, Retire, Relocate); assessment via Migration Evaluator (TCO plus dependency analysis) and AWS Application Discovery Service; wave planning in Migration Hub; tracking through Migration Hub Strategy Recommendations; AWS Professional Services or Partners for large engagements
Lift-and-shift everything indiscriminately to EC2 to maximize migration speed — the operational improvement from cloud-native services is offset by the migration delay of refactoring
questions.explanation:
Migration framework: (1) **7 Rs** — for each app decide: Rehost (lift-and-shift), Replatform (managed services replacement), Refactor (code changes), Repurchase (switch to SaaS), Retain (keep on-prem), Retire (kill unused), Relocate (e.g., VMware Cloud on AWS for VMware shops). (2) **Assess** — Migration Evaluator (TCO comparison, on-prem footprint analysis), Application Discovery Service (agent-based + agentless data collection), Migration Hub Strategy Recommendations (auto-categorize apps by R). (3) **Plan waves** — group related apps; sequence migrations; minimize cross-wave dependencies. (4) **Migration Hub** — central tracking (status per app, per wave). (5) **Execute** — MGN (VMs), DMS (DBs), DataSync (files), App2Container (containerize), Snow Family (offline). (6) **Validate** — load testing, security scanning, runbooks. (7) **Optimize** — post-migration, right-size + Spot/Savings Plans + serverless modernization. (8) **Migration Acceleration Program (MAP)** — funded engagement model with AWS / partners for large migrations.

Option B misses opportunity for cost/perf optimization. Option C is too aggressive (slow). Option D is reckless. The trap: candidates may not know the 7 Rs or Migration Hub Strategy Recommendations.
questions.pattern: Migration phases: Assess (discovery, TCO, R-classification). Mobilize (foundational AWS setup, training, quick wins). Migrate & Modernize (waves, tooling). Each app: Rehost → Replatform → Refactor as appropriate over time (don't refactor at migration if it delays value).

questions.scenario: A company has a hybrid setup post-migration: some workloads in AWS, some on-prem permanently. They need consistent management, monitoring, and software deployments across both environments.
questions.question: Which AWS approach unifies HYBRID OPERATIONS?
choices: Ignore on-prem entirely and let the on-prem team manage their own tools without integration — separation of concerns produces the cleanest hybrid architecture
Use only on-prem tools for both environments — on-prem tools work on EC2 instances when the SSM agent is disabled, providing unified management without AWS-specific services

AWS Systems Manager hybrid activations (manage on-prem and EC2 with SSM agent — Run Command, Patch Manager, Session Manager, Inventory); AWS Outposts (AWS hardware on-prem extending AWS APIs); EKS Anywhere (Kubernetes on-prem, AWS-supported); ECS Anywhere (run ECS tasks on customer-managed infra); CloudWatch Agent on on-prem hosts; AWS Resilience Hub for cross-environment posture; AWS Config conformance packs across hybrid via Config aggregator
Manage on-prem and AWS with completely separate tools — separate tooling per environment provides better focus and avoids the complexity of unified management
questions.explanation:
Hybrid operations on AWS: (1) **SSM Hybrid Activations** — install SSM agent on on-prem servers; treat them as managed instances; run commands, patch, audit, Session Manager (no SSH key management), Inventory (software/hardware), Compliance, Distributor (deploy software). (2) **Outposts** — AWS-owned hardware in customer data center; runs AWS services (EC2, EBS, S3, RDS, etc.) with same APIs; for low-latency or data-residency needs. (3) **EKS Anywhere** — open-source Kubernetes on-prem; AWS-supported; integrate with AWS for upstream services. (4) **ECS Anywhere** — run ECS tasks on customer-managed infrastructure (VMs, bare metal); central orchestration. (5) **CloudWatch Agent** on on-prem for unified metrics/logs. (6) **AWS Config + Conformance Packs** for compliance across hybrid via Config aggregator. (7) **Direct Connect / VPN** for connectivity. (8) **Resilience Hub** scores resilience across hybrid workloads. (9) **AWS Well-Architected hybrid lens** for guidance.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may not know AWS extends out to on-prem with first-class managed services.
questions.pattern: Hybrid options: Outposts (AWS hardware on-prem). EKS/ECS Anywhere (containers). SSM (any servers). Direct Connect (private network). Storage Gateway (hybrid storage). Local Zones / Wavelength (low-latency edge — different but related). Use AWS API surface uniformly across cloud and edge to reduce operational divergence.

questions.scenario: A multinational has separate AWS Organizations in different geographies (EU, US, APAC) due to historical M&A. Leadership wants a single bill, unified governance, and shared services — without merging into one Org (which is irreversible and disruptive).
questions.question: Which approach is MOST APPROPRIATE?
choices: Use AWS Organizations' merge-organizations API to combine the two parent and subsidiary Orgs into one — Organizations supports atomic merges with all SCPs and OUs preserved
Configure AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM) to share the parent Organization's structure with the subsidiary Organization — RAM's organization-share feature unifies the two Orgs

Keep separate Organizations, use Cross-account IAM roles for shared services access, separate billing per Organization, and aggregate usage with Cost and Usage Reports landing in a single S3 bucket queried via Athena and QuickSight; AWS Organizations does not support merging two existing Orgs
Move all subsidiary accounts out of their current Organization and re-invite them to the parent Organization in a single coordinated operation, accepting brief disruption to consolidated billing
questions.explanation:
AWS Organizations cannot be nested or federated — each account belongs to exactly one Org at a time. Realistic approaches when leadership wants unification but full merger isn't viable: (1) Keep separate Orgs but ship Cost and Usage Reports (CUR) to a single S3 bucket, query with Athena, visualize in QuickSight for unified financial visibility (no actual billing merger, but operational visibility). (2) Cross-account IAM roles let workloads in one Org consume shared services in another. (3) For full unification, migration via leave + invite is possible but disruptive — accounts move one at a time with brief windows.

Migration option is technically possible but the scenario explicitly says 'without merging' — it IS merging. Control Tower has no multi-Org mode (fictional). Orgs cannot be nested (fictional). The trap: candidates may invent capabilities that sound plausible.
questions.pattern: Org structure facts: one Org per account at a time. Migration possible via leave/invite (disruptive). For unification without merging: cross-account IAM roles, centralized CUR in shared S3, federated identity, RAM cross-Org sharing (limited). Full merge typically pursued when scale + governance benefits outweigh disruption cost.

questions.scenario: A company has 50 VPCs across 5 regions. They need east-west traffic between VPCs in any region, traffic inspection at a central firewall, and selective access from on-prem.
questions.question: Which networking architecture is APPROPRIATE?
choices: Amazon Route 53 Resolver Forwarding Rules across all regions — Resolver's cross-region rule sharing enables multi-region private DNS that replaces the need for a managed global network
VPC peering between every region with TGW in each region for intra-region routing — this mesh provides equivalent global connectivity to Cloud WAN at lower cost

AWS Cloud WAN — managed global network with policy-driven segmentation across regions, integrates with Transit Gateways, supports central inspection via Network Firewall in inspection VPCs; alternative is multiple TGWs interconnected via TGW peering for smaller global networks
AWS Direct Connect Gateway with global VIFs — DX Gateway extends a single Direct Connect across all regions and provides the policy-driven segmentation that Cloud WAN otherwise handles
questions.explanation:
Multi-region multi-VPC at scale needs purpose-built solutions: (1) AWS Cloud WAN — managed global network with policy-driven segments (defined in JSON) spanning regions; integrates with TGWs; supports inspection patterns. Best for 50+ VPC global topologies. (2) Per-region Transit Gateways peered across regions — older but proven pattern; route tables segment traffic; centralize traffic inspection in dedicated inspection VPCs (Network Firewall, Gateway Load Balancer + 3rd-party firewalls); on-prem via Direct Connect Gateway.

Peering doesn't scale (1225 pairs for 50 VPCs); inter-region peering creates a fragile mesh. VPCs cannot span regions — fundamentally wrong. Full-mesh VPN over EC2 is inefficient and operationally painful. The trap: 'mega-VPC' sounds elegant but VPCs are regional resources.
questions.pattern: Multi-region networking: Cloud WAN (managed, policy-driven, global). TGW + TGW peering (regional hubs + cross-region links). Inspection VPC (centralized firewalling via GWLB). Direct Connect Gateway (multi-region from on-prem). VPC Lattice (application-layer cross-VPC). Choose by topology size and operational preference.

questions.scenario: A regulated company must demonstrate that workloads in production never communicate with the public internet — neither inbound nor outbound — except via approved egress points where traffic is logged and inspected.
questions.question: Which AWS architecture ENFORCES this?
choices: Place a NAT Gateway in each VPC with strict route tables — NAT Gateway inspection mode filters egress traffic at the application layer and replaces the need for Network Firewall
Use AWS WAF in front of every VPC to inspect outbound traffic — WAF's outbound-filtering feature handles the central egress inspection with full L7 visibility into requests leaving the AWS network

Allow Internet Gateway on each VPC and rely on egress security groups to block unwanted outbound traffic — security groups inspect all egress at L4 and prevent unauthorized internet access without needing a central inspection layer

VPCs in private subnets only (no IGW), VPC endpoints (Gateway for S3/DynamoDB, Interface PrivateLink for AWS services) so traffic stays on the AWS network; controlled egress through a central inspection VPC running AWS Network Firewall; SCPs deny IGW attachment org-wide
questions.explanation:
Strict no-internet posture requires: private subnets only (no IGW means no inbound from internet); VPC endpoints (Gateway for S3/DynamoDB free, Interface PrivateLink for AWS services); egress inspection VPC with AWS Network Firewall (stateful, FQDN/SNI filtering, IDS/IPS); preventive guardrails via SCPs denying ec2:CreateInternetGateway/CreateNatGateway outside approved OUs; detective via Config rules and VPC Flow Logs.

Public subnets with security groups still has IGW present — fails 'no public internet'. iptables on EC2 doesn't scale and is fragile. IGW with NACLs is whack-a-mole; NACLs are stateless and have rule limits. The trap: candidates may know individual pieces but miss the SCP-level prevention layer.
questions.pattern: No-internet VPC posture: private subnets, VPC endpoints (S3/DDB Gateway + others Interface). Egress inspection VPC with Network Firewall. SCPs prevent IGW/NAT creation. Config detective. VPC Flow Logs centralized. Route 53 Resolver query logging. Defense in depth at every layer.

questions.scenario: A central platform team in account A operates an internal API. Multiple consumer accounts (B, C, D, ...) need private access to it. Direct VPC peering would expose all of A's network to consumers and grant consumers more access than the API.
questions.question: Which AWS service is PURPOSE-BUILT for this private service-exposure pattern?
choices: AWS Transit Gateway shared via RAM with all consumer accounts — TGW handles the cross-account private exposure of the single API while keeping the rest of account A's services unreachable
AWS Direct Connect with private VIFs from each consumer account to account A — DX private VIFs expose only the specific API endpoint and isolate the rest of account A's resources
VPC peering between account A's VPC and each consumer account's VPC — peering routes the API traffic privately and the security groups in account A control which consumers can reach the API

AWS PrivateLink — expose the API behind a Network Load Balancer in account A as a VPC endpoint service; consumers create Interface VPC Endpoints in their accounts pointing at the service; only the specific service is exposed (no broad VPC reachability); supports private DNS
questions.explanation:
AWS PrivateLink is purpose-built for one-way private service exposure: producer side hosts service behind NLB or GWLB and exposes via VPC endpoint service; consumer side creates Interface VPC Endpoint pointing at producer. Consumer's traffic reaches producer over PrivateLink; producer cannot reach back into consumer's VPC. Only the specific service ports/IPs are exposed. No CIDR conflicts matter. No route changes needed.

Transit Gateway exposes more than necessary and creates routing complexity. VPC peering exposes all of A's network. Public endpoint defeats 'private access' requirement. The trap: candidates may default to TGW for any cross-VPC need; PrivateLink is specifically the right tool for service exposure.
questions.pattern: Cross-VPC connectivity decision: peering (general L3, exposes full networks). TGW (hub-and-spoke L3, scales). PrivateLink (service-specific exposure, one-way, no CIDR conflicts). VPC Lattice (app-layer with auth/observability). Choose narrowest scope that fits — PrivateLink is the cleanest for service exposure across account boundaries.

questions.scenario: A company's CloudTrail Organization Trail logs 100 million events per day. The security team wants to query these efficiently for incident investigations — sometimes searching across years of history.
questions.question: Which AWS approach is MOST APPROPRIATE for ad-hoc CloudTrail querying at scale?
choices: S3 server access logs with Athena queries against the central log bucket — server access logs include the full CloudTrail event stream and are SQL-queryable directly from Athena
AWS Config aggregator with the audit-event extension — Config's aggregator stores 10 years of CloudTrail data and exposes a SQL-like advanced query interface natively
Amazon CloudWatch Logs Insights against the CloudTrail log group — Logs Insights handles ad-hoc queries across years of CloudTrail data and is the AWS-recommended audit query interface

CloudTrail Lake — managed audit data lake with SQL-queryable interface, flexible retention up to 10 years, supports multi-account and multi-region aggregation, federated queries across CloudTrail and Config; replaces the older approach of dumping CloudTrail to S3 and querying via Athena
questions.explanation:
CloudTrail Lake is the AWS-native audit data lake: captures CloudTrail events (and Config items, plus audit logs from supported services); retention up to 10 years; SQL interface (PartiQL); fast queries across years; multi-account/region Org-wide aggregation; federation across event data stores. Athena alternative — for very large scale (petabytes), ship logs to S3, register in Glue, query with Athena; cheaper per query but you operate the table maintenance and partitioning.

OpenSearch + Kinesis Firehose works but high ingestion cost at this volume; better for shorter-retention real-time analytics. Console UI is limited to 90 days, not multi-year search. QuickSight is BI dashboarding, not raw event search. The trap: candidates may know OpenSearch for log analytics generically but miss CloudTrail Lake.
questions.pattern: CloudTrail query options: console (90 days, ad hoc). CloudTrail Lake (managed, multi-year, SQL). Athena on S3 logs (cheap at scale, more ops). OpenSearch (real-time, expensive at high volume, rich UI). Security Lake (broader OCSF-format security data). Choose by retention, query frequency, scale.

questions.scenario: A company's CFO wants chargeback per business unit across all 200 AWS accounts: each BU should see and pay for its actual usage. Today, the bill is one big invoice with no clear attribution.
questions.question: Which approach BEST enables chargeback?
choices: Move each BU to its own AWS Organization and let each BU pay AWS directly — separate Organizations provide the cleanest chargeback because each BU sees only its own bill

Mandate BU tags on every resource (Tag Policies enforce); enable cost allocation tags; ship the Cost and Usage Report (CUR) to a central S3 bucket; query via Athena and visualize in QuickSight for per-BU dashboards; supplement with AWS Cost Categories for BU-level rollups; integrate with AWS Billing Conductor for showback/chargeback billing
Configure AWS Budgets per resource and rely on the budget alerts to allocate cost to BUs — budget-based attribution is more accurate than tag-based because it captures usage in real time
Use AWS Cost Anomaly Detection to attribute spend per BU — anomaly detection's BU-level allocation is built into the service and replaces the need for tagging or CUR
questions.explanation:
Comprehensive chargeback architecture combines: tagging (mandatory tags BU/CostCenter/Project/Owner enforced via Tag Policies + SCPs); cost allocation tags activated in Billing console; Cost and Usage Report delivered to central S3 (granular per-resource hourly); Athena queries; QuickSight dashboards (showback or formal chargeback); Cost Categories for rule-based grouping (by account, service, tag, charge type); Cost Anomaly Detection per BU; Budgets per BU.

Consolidated billing alone with manual extraction doesn't scale and lacks resource granularity. Cost Categories alone helps with grouping but without tagging discipline you can't attribute below the account level. Separate payer accounts loses Organization-wide volume discounts and Reserved Instance/Savings Plans sharing. The trap: tagging discipline is the foundation; tools sit on top.
questions.pattern: Chargeback foundation: tags (mandatory, enforced). CUR → S3 → Athena → QuickSight. Cost Categories for grouping. Budgets + Anomaly Detection per BU. Showback (visibility) → chargeback (formal billback). Cultural: FinOps team, regular reviews. AWS Billing Conductor (in some scenarios) for custom pricing/markups.

questions.scenario: A company runs Amazon EKS clusters in 5 accounts. They want to share container images centrally — pull from one ECR registry across all accounts without duplicating images.
questions.question: Which approach ENABLES centralized ECR sharing?
choices: ECR repository policies grant cross-account pull permissions; use ECR pull-through cache to keep frequently-pulled public images cached privately; for pre-built internal images, ECR Replication can mirror to multiple regions or accounts; combine with image scanning (Inspector) and image signing (cosign) for supply-chain security
Copy images via S3 to each consumer account where they re-import to local ECR — the S3-based pattern is more flexible than ECR cross-account access and supports any image registry format
Use AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM) to share the ECR repository with all consumer accounts — RAM's container-registry-share feature handles cross-account pulls with audit and signing built in

Make the ECR repository public and rely on the trust boundary of the AWS account ID to control who pulls images — public repos are scanned by AWS automatically and the access is auditable via CloudTrail
questions.explanation:
Centralized ECR architecture: repository policies grant cross-account read (BatchGetImage, GetDownloadUrlForLayer) to consumer principals or Org IDs. Consumers reference the central registry's URL when pulling. ECR Replication automatically copies images to other regions/accounts (proximity, DR). Pull-through cache mirrors public registries (Docker Hub, GHCR, etc.) into private ECR — controls supply chain, avoids public rate limits. Inspector scans for vulnerabilities. Image signing via ECR + AWS Signer.

S3 + wget bypasses container-registry semantics (layers, manifests, signing). Nightly Lambda is fragile, lags, and duplicates images instead of using native replication. Docker Hub introduces a 3rd-party dependency and rate limits. The trap: candidates may invent custom replication when ECR has native cross-account features.
questions.pattern: Container registry sharing: repository policies (cross-account read). ECR Replication (cross-region/account copies). Pull-through cache (public registries). Inspector (scanning). Image signing (Signer). For pull credentials in EKS: IRSA. For image promotion across environments: tag-based or registry-based promotion in CI/CD.

questions.scenario: A company's developers create dozens of feature branches per week, each needing an isolated test environment with its own database, infrastructure, and DNS hostname. Manually provisioning takes hours.
questions.question: Which AWS approach BEST enables on-demand ephemeral environments?
choices: Configure AWS Application Composer with the auto-environment feature — Composer creates and destroys environments per PR automatically based on Git webhook events
Maintain one always-on dev environment with feature flags toggled per developer — feature flags isolate developer changes and avoid the cost of provisioning ephemeral environments

Provision-on-demand pattern: IaC (CloudFormation, CDK, Terraform) parameterized by branch name; CI pipeline triggers stack creation per PR (Account Vending Machine for full account isolation, or per-branch stacks within a shared dev account); tear down on PR merge or close; combine with Service Catalog for parameterized templates
Use AWS Cloud9 environments per developer — Cloud9 provides full ephemeral environment isolation including the underlying AWS resources for each developer's branch
questions.explanation:
Ephemeral environment architecture: IaC parameterized by branch (CloudFormation/CDK/Terraform stack named after branch). CI pipeline runs cdk deploy on PR open and writes the resulting endpoint URL back to the PR. PR close triggers automatic teardown. Account strategy: dedicated 'sandbox' account or Account Vending Machine creates throwaway accounts (full isolation; nukes via account closure). Route 53 wildcard records like *.feature.example.com enable per-branch subdomains. Service Catalog provides curated 'menu' of approved templates. Amplify for full-stack web apps with branch deployments built-in. Cost control: TTL tags, scheduled cleanup of stale stacks, budget alarms.

Auto Scaling doesn't isolate environments — still shared. Shared staging causes test conflicts. WorkSpaces are dev IDEs, not production-like test environments. The trap: candidates may not know AVM or branch-specific stack patterns.
questions.pattern: Ephemeral environments: IaC + CI = per-branch stacks. Account-level isolation for full clean (AVM) or stack-level (cheaper, faster). DNS wildcards for routing. Self-service via Service Catalog. Cost via TTLs + budgets. CDK is particularly suited (typed, testable, programmatic).

questions.scenario: A new application processes financial transactions and must be highly available (99.99% target). The team is choosing the database tier — they need ACID transactions with multi-AZ resilience, automated failover under 1 minute, and cross-region disaster recovery.
questions.question: Which AWS database is APPROPRIATE?
choices: Self-managed PostgreSQL on EC2 with cross-region streaming replication via WAL shipping — this configuration provides better RPO/RTO than Aurora Global because the team controls the replication topology
Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL with Multi-AZ and a manual cross-region read replica — manual promotion of the cross-region replica achieves RPO/RTO equivalent to Aurora Global at lower cost
Amazon DynamoDB with Global Tables — Global Tables' active-active replication handles the relational ACID workload at sub-second latency between regions

Amazon Aurora with Multi-AZ deployment (synchronous replicas) and automated failover; for cross-region DR use Aurora Global Database (sub-second cross-region replication, RPO <1s, RTO ~1min for managed failover); read replicas for read scaling; backtrack/PITR for fast recovery from logical corruption
questions.explanation:
Aurora is the AWS-native HA/DR relational solution: Multi-AZ uses distributed storage replicated 6 ways across 3 AZs; failover under a minute (often ~30s); no app code changes (DNS endpoint flips). Up to 15 read replicas with sub-10ms lag, can be promoted. Aurora Global Database has primary writer in one region, replica clusters in up to 5 other regions; cross-region replication < 1 second; RTO ~1 minute for managed planned failover, faster for unplanned. Backtrack (MySQL flavor) quickly rewinds cluster state without restoring from backup.

DynamoDB is single-record-transactional (or limited multi-item via TransactWriteItems); not the right fit for complex relational/transactional workloads. Self-managed clustering is fragile and operational burden; EBS Multi-Attach has serious caveats (no concurrent write filesystem support). SQL Server Std edition has limited HA features; cross-region replicas are async; manual promotion takes longer. The trap: candidates may know individual options but not Aurora Global Database specifics.
questions.pattern: Relational HA/DR on AWS: RDS Multi-AZ (single-region HA). RDS cross-region read replicas (slower DR). Aurora Multi-AZ (faster failover, distributed storage). Aurora Global Database (cross-region with near-zero RPO). For multi-region active-active SQL: Aurora DSQL (newer). Pick the simplest service that meets RTO/RPO.

questions.scenario: A new IoT platform ingests telemetry from 5 million devices: small frequent messages (~200 bytes), needs MQTT, device authentication via X.509 certificates, fine-grained per-device authorization, and downstream routing to S3 for analytics and DynamoDB for real-time state.
questions.question: Which AWS service is APPROPRIATE for the ingestion + routing layer?
choices: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with each device as a producer using AWS SigV4 signed PUT calls — Kinesis directly handles 1M+ producers and the integrated rules engine routes to downstream services
Amazon MQ ActiveMQ broker on EC2 with per-device certificate authentication — managed ActiveMQ provides the MQTT protocol support and the routing rules required for IoT ingestion
Amazon API Gateway WebSocket APIs with per-device API keys for authentication — WebSocket scales to 1M+ devices and the API Gateway's built-in routing handles fan-out to downstream services

AWS IoT Core — managed MQTT broker, X.509 certificate auth (per-device), fine-grained policies (resource-level), Rules Engine for routing to S3/DynamoDB/Kinesis/Lambda/Timestream/SNS without custom code; supports 1M+ devices; pair with Device Defender for anomaly detection
questions.explanation:
AWS IoT Core is the purpose-built managed MQTT broker for IoT scale. Protocols: MQTT, MQTT over WSS, HTTPS. Auth: X.509 certificates registered per-device (or just-in-time provisioning for fleet onboarding); IAM-based or AWS-IoT-policy-based authorization at fine granularity. Rules Engine routes messages to S3/DynamoDB/Lambda/Kinesis/Timestream/SNS/SQS/OpenSearch via SQL-like statements with no custom code. Device Shadows hold JSON desired/reported state per device (offline scenarios). IoT Device Defender provides auditing + anomaly detection. IoT Greengrass for edge runtime.

API Gateway WebSocket isn't MQTT and lacks IoT-specific features (shadows, defender). Self-managed Mosquitto on ECS reintroduces operational burden. Amazon MQ is for traditional message broker workloads (RabbitMQ, ActiveMQ), not optimized for IoT scale or built-in IoT features. The trap: candidates may know API Gateway WebSocket and try to extend it to IoT scenarios.
questions.pattern: IoT architecture on AWS: IoT Core (broker, rules, shadows). Greengrass (edge). Device Defender (security). FleetWise (auto/connected vehicles). SiteWise (industrial). For analytics: stream to Kinesis, Timestream (time-series), S3 + Athena, OpenSearch. For ML inference at edge: Greengrass + SageMaker Edge.

questions.scenario: A company designs a new gaming backend requiring real-time multiplayer state sync between players across the globe, with sub-100ms latency for the same game session.
questions.question: Which AWS architecture is MOST APPROPRIATE for low-latency multiplayer game state?
choices: Use Amazon WorkSpaces with the gaming protocol enabled — WorkSpaces' streaming infrastructure provides the low-latency game state synchronization across regions for multiplayer matches

Amazon GameLift for managed game-server hosting (place sessions in regions close to matched players, FleetIQ for cost-optimized Spot+On-Demand mix); pair with Global Accelerator for anycast UDP/TCP to closest healthy region with rapid failover; DynamoDB Global Tables for player state
Run game servers on AWS Lambda with WebSocket APIs — Lambda's per-invocation pricing scales to any player count and the WebSocket APIs handle multiplayer state at single-digit ms latency
Run game servers on EC2 across all regions behind Application Load Balancers — ALBs provide the latency-based routing for game traffic with built-in health checks
questions.explanation:
Multiplayer gaming architecture: GameLift provides managed game server hosting with FlexMatch matchmaking; FleetIQ optimizes Spot+On-Demand mix for cost; sessions placed in regions near matched players. Global Accelerator anycast routes players to nearest game server over AWS backbone (UDP/TCP, lower latency than public internet). State storage: ElastiCache Redis or MemoryDB (durable Redis) for fast hot state; DynamoDB for persistent player progression. Cognito User Pools for identity. Real-time messaging within session via game server broadcasts.

DynamoDB with 1-second TTL polling at 100ms can't deliver sub-100ms multiplayer; DynamoDB isn't latency-optimized for active game frames. S3 PutObject is far too slow (hundreds of ms). Lambda + Aurora at 30Hz is wildly expensive and not low-latency. The trap: candidates may know DynamoDB/S3/Lambda for general use but not the gaming-specific stack.
questions.pattern: Real-time low-latency on AWS: Global Accelerator (anycast TCP/UDP). GameLift (game-specific managed hosting). ElastiCache/MemoryDB (sub-ms state). For browser-to-server: WebSocket via API Gateway or AppSync subscriptions. For machine-to-machine real-time: Kinesis or MSK. Match technology to the latency budget.

questions.scenario: A new B2B SaaS application supports tenants with very different traffic profiles: some send 100K req/sec, others 1 req/min. The team needs strong tenant isolation, fair use, and per-tenant cost visibility.
questions.question: Which design pattern is APPROPRIATE for multi-tenant SaaS at this scale?
choices: Apply a tiering strategy informed by AWS SaaS Factory guidance: silo high-volume / high-compliance tenants in dedicated accounts or dedicated stacks; pool small tenants in shared infrastructure with row-level / namespace-level isolation; combine with tenant-aware throttling, per-tenant cost allocation tags, and per-tenant data encryption keys
Pool every tenant into one shared monolithic application database with tenant_id columns — the simplest possible schema scales to any tenant count via DynamoDB's auto-sharding

Provision a separate AWS account per tenant for complete isolation — every tenant gets a dedicated account with full multi-AZ infrastructure, providing the strongest possible isolation regardless of tenant size
Use Amazon WorkSpaces to give each tenant a dedicated virtual desktop — WorkSpaces' isolation model is the AWS-recommended SaaS multi-tenancy pattern at any scale
questions.explanation:
Multi-tenant SaaS at scale (per AWS SaaS Factory) uses a tiering strategy: full silo (dedicated account/stack — high cost, max isolation, fits high-tier or compliance tenants); pool (shared infra, tenant_id filtering — low cost, scales, fits small tenants); hybrid — silo high-volume tenants, pool the rest. Isolation via IAM policies with tenant_id condition keys, row-level filters in databases, dynamic IAM session policies. Cognito User Pools (per-tenant or shared with custom claim). Throttling via API Gateway usage plans per tenant tier. Cost attribution via tags + CUR + Athena.

Pool-only fails for noisy-neighbor heavy tenants. Silo-everything is wildly expensive at this tenant count. Single Lambda with switch logic doesn't address the architectural tiering question. The trap: candidates may know either pattern in isolation but miss the hybrid tiered approach.
questions.pattern: SaaS tenant strategies: silo (dedicated, max isolation, high cost). Pool (shared, low cost, isolation by code). Bridge (silo per tier — premium silo, basic pool). Identity: Cognito (per-tenant pool or shared with claim). Tagging + cost attribution. SaaS Boost / SaaS Factory provide reference architectures.

questions.scenario: A new application deployed in EKS must call AWS APIs (S3, DynamoDB, Bedrock). The team wants the cleanest, least-privilege identity model — no instance-profile leakage, no static IAM users, per-pod identity.
questions.question: Which approach is MOST APPROPRIATE on EKS?
choices: Create a single Kubernetes Secret containing AWS access keys that all pods mount as a volume — Secrets are encrypted in etcd and rotated quarterly, providing equivalent security to IAM-role-per-pod
Use Kubernetes ServiceAccount tokens directly to call AWS APIs — modern AWS SDKs accept Kubernetes ServiceAccount tokens natively as a credential source without needing IRSA or Pod Identity

EKS Pod Identity (or IRSA — IAM Roles for Service Accounts) — map a Kubernetes ServiceAccount to an IAM role; pods using that ServiceAccount automatically receive scoped temporary credentials via the AWS SDK; no node-level instance profile leakage; deny pod usage of node identity via pod security standards
Attach a broad IAM role to the EKS worker node group covering all pod permissions — node-level credentials are accessed via IMDSv2 by all pods on the node and provide unified credential management
questions.explanation:
Modern per-pod identity on EKS: EKS Pod Identity (newer, recommended) installs the EKS Pod Identity Agent and associates IAM roles with Kubernetes ServiceAccounts; pod consumes temporary credentials via the SDK transparently. Simpler than IRSA. IRSA (older but widely used) uses OIDC provider; SA annotation links to IAM role; AWS SDK assumes role automatically. Both eliminate node-level credential leakage. Each ServiceAccount gets only the permissions its pods need (s3:GetObject on specific bucket, dynamodb:GetItem on specific table). CloudTrail logs every assumption.

Secrets in pods are anti-pattern (rotation is manual, leak risk). Node-level instance profile leaks credentials to all pods on the node. Long-lived keys in container images is a security incident waiting to happen. The trap: candidates may know IRSA but not Pod Identity.
questions.pattern: EKS workload identity: Pod Identity (newest, simplest). IRSA (proven, OIDC-based). Avoid: instance profile sharing, static credentials in images, K8s Secrets with AWS keys. For ECS: task roles. For Lambda: execution role. For EC2: instance profile. Universal pattern: AWS service consumers get scoped IAM roles via temp credentials.

questions.scenario: A new analytical workload runs Spark jobs that need to read 50 TB from S3, transform, and write back. Cost-effectiveness is a top requirement; jobs run nightly and can tolerate interruption.
questions.question: Which AWS configuration MOST optimizes cost for this workload?
choices: Run EMR on a single large On-Demand x2idn instance for predictable performance — large instances avoid the inter-node coordination overhead of distributed Spark and reduce total compute time

EMR Serverless or EMR on EC2 with managed scaling, using Spot Instances with capacity-optimized allocation strategy (diverse instance types/families across multiple subnets), and Graviton-based instances for additional 20-40% price-performance gains; combine with S3 Intelligent-Tiering for the input/output data
Use AWS Lambda with the Spark runtime layer — Lambda's per-invocation pricing is more cost-effective for any Spark workload than EMR with Spot
Run the workload on Amazon EC2 Reserved Instances purchased for 3 years — RIs provide the deepest discount available on AWS and outperform Spot for analytics workloads
questions.explanation:
Cost-optimized batch Spark: EMR Serverless or EMR on EC2 with managed scaling — both auto-scale workers; Serverless eliminates cluster lifecycle, pay per second; EC2 EMR gives more control. Spot Instances up to 90% discount; capacity-optimized allocation chooses Spot pools with most spare capacity (lowest interruption); diversify across instance types/families/subnets. Graviton (ARM64) typically 20-40% better price-perf for Spark. Small On-Demand core nodes for HDFS, shuffle survival; Spot task nodes for transient compute. S3-optimized committers + EMRFS for efficient writes (avoid rename-based commits).

24/7 cluster wastes money during off-hours. Glue doesn't have 3-year reserved DPU commitments. Single instance bottlenecks throughput and lacks distributed Spark benefits. The trap: candidates may know Spot but not capacity-optimized vs lowest-price strategies.
questions.pattern: Big-data cost optimization: serverless when possible (EMR Serverless, Athena, Glue). Spot diversification. Graviton instances. Right-size workers. Right table format (Iceberg/Delta). Right file format (Parquet, ZSTD). Predicate pushdown. Partition pruning. Cold storage tiering for older data.

questions.scenario: A new application stores 50 million customer records, each containing PII. Regulatory requirements: encrypt with customer-managed keys, key rotation annually, access auditable, ability to revoke decryption from compromised principals quickly.
questions.question: Which AWS KMS pattern is APPROPRIATE?
choices: Use the AWS-managed KMS key (aws/s3) for all data — AWS-managed keys provide the same encryption strength as customer-managed keys and remove the rotation and policy management burden
Use a single customer-managed KMS key for all data categories — sharing one key across all workloads simplifies key management and the IAM-based access control covers per-category restrictions
Encrypt data client-side with a hardcoded application secret — client-side encryption avoids KMS API costs and the application secret can be rotated as part of normal deployments

Customer-managed KMS keys (CMK) per data category (one for PII, one for financial); enable automatic annual key rotation; key policy restricts decrypt to specific IAM roles/services with condition keys (aws:SourceVpce, kms:ViaService); use KMS Multi-Region keys for cross-region workloads; CloudTrail logs every key use
questions.explanation:
Production KMS architecture: customer-managed CMK (not AWS-managed) gives full control over policy, rotation, deletion. Per-data-category keys provide separation of duties and limit blast radius. Annual rotation enabled (automatic); old key versions retained for decryption of historical data. Key policy restricts to specific IAM roles/services using condition keys like kms:ViaService (only via specific AWS service) and kms:EncryptionContext (bind to specific user_id). CloudTrail logs every Encrypt/Decrypt call. Revocation by removing principal from key policy takes effect for new requests immediately. Multi-region keys for cross-region replication. Envelope encryption: per-object data keys via GenerateDataKey, only the small data key is KMS-encrypted; bulk data encrypted client-side (high throughput, low cost).

AWS-managed keys can't be policy-customized; can't be revoked from specific principals. App-embedded keys are an anti-pattern. Single key + broad policy violates least privilege.
questions.pattern: KMS production patterns: customer-managed keys per category. Automatic rotation. Tight key policies + condition keys. Envelope encryption for bulk. CloudTrail audit. Multi-region keys for cross-region. Service-specific helpers (S3 SSE-KMS, EBS KMS, RDS KMS, DynamoDB encryption at rest with CMK).

questions.scenario: A new microservice needs background job processing: jobs arrive via API, take 10-30 seconds, must be processed exactly once, with retry on failure and a poison-message queue for unprocessable jobs.
questions.question: Which AWS architecture is APPROPRIATE?
choices: API Gateway → Lambda → S3 events → Lambda consumer — S3's event-notification model provides reliable delivery with at-least-once semantics equivalent to SQS

API Gateway → Lambda producer → SQS FIFO queue (or Standard with idempotency in consumer) → Lambda or ECS/Fargate consumer with DLQ (dead-letter queue) for messages that fail max receives; configure visibility timeout aligned with consumer processing time; CloudWatch alarms on queue depth and DLQ messages
API Gateway directly integrated with DynamoDB via service integration; DynamoDB Streams trigger Lambda consumers — this synchronous pattern provides the strongest consistency guarantees
API Gateway → Step Functions Express workflow → Lambda — Step Functions handles the queuing, retries, and DLQ functionality natively without needing SQS
questions.explanation:
Background job processing with SQS: Standard (high throughput, at-least-once, no order) or FIFO (ordered, exactly-once-by-message-group, lower throughput). API Gateway → Lambda → SQS SendMessage. Consumer is Lambda (event source mapping with batch size and concurrency) or ECS/Fargate (long-running pull workers). Visibility timeout set longer than max processing time.
