Retries via re-delivery after visibility timeout expires without delete; max receives before going to DLQ. DLQ separate queue with CloudWatch alarm on depth + automated triage. Idempotency: dedupe on business key with DynamoDB conditional write (PutItem with ConditionExpression attribute_not_exists(jobId)). Lambda Powertools has idempotency utilities.

Concurrency=1 destroys throughput. DynamoDB-based custom queue is over-engineered when SQS exists. SNS fanout to multiple SQS queues is wrong pattern (you'd do multiple deliveries, not one).
questions.pattern: Async job pattern: producer → SQS → consumer → optional DLQ. Idempotent consumers (DynamoDB conditional writes). Visibility timeout > processing time. Monitor DLQ depth. For ordering: SQS FIFO. For fanout: SNS → multiple SQS. For event-driven cross-service: EventBridge.

questions.scenario: A new internal API used by 10 backend services needs centralized authentication, rate limiting per consumer, request validation, and observability — without each consumer doing it themselves.
questions.question: Which AWS service architecture FITS?
choices: Application Load Balancer with target group rules — ALB's listener rules handle per-consumer authentication, rate limiting, request validation, and provide the unified API surface that API Gateway would offer
AWS App Runner with the API mode enabled — App Runner provides managed API hosting with built-in auth, rate limiting, and request validation, replacing the need for API Gateway

Amazon API Gateway (REST or HTTP API) — IAM/SigV4 for service-to-service auth (or Cognito/Lambda authorizers for richer auth); usage plans with API keys for per-consumer rate limiting; request validation; private APIs accessible only from VPC via Interface Endpoints; integrated with WAF, X-Ray, and CloudWatch
Amazon CloudFront with Lambda@Edge for authentication — CloudFront's edge functions provide per-request auth and rate limiting at lower cost than API Gateway
questions.explanation:
API Gateway is the managed front-door for APIs. REST API has feature-rich support (request/response transforms, request validators, caching, mock integrations); higher per-request cost. HTTP API is simpler, faster, cheaper; supports JWT/OIDC authorizers natively; recommended for new APIs unless you need REST features. Auth: IAM (SigV4, ideal for AWS service-to-service), Cognito User Pools, JWT (HTTP API), Lambda authorizers (custom logic), API keys + usage plans (rate limiting per consumer). Throttling per-method or per-key. Request schema validation rejects malformed early. Observability: CloudWatch metrics, access logs to CloudWatch/Kinesis, X-Ray tracing. Private endpoints (PrivateLink) for internal-only.

ALB + custom Lambda authorizers can be built but reinvents the wheel and lacks usage-plan-based rate limiting natively. Each service rolling its own is exactly what API Gateway prevents (consistency, observability, less work). The trap: candidates may know API Gateway exists but not its specific features.
questions.pattern: API gateway choice: API Gateway HTTP API (default for new). API Gateway REST (when REST features needed). AppSync (GraphQL). ALB (if API GW features unnecessary, simpler). For internal high-throughput service mesh: VPC Lattice or App Mesh. Layer: WAF + CloudFront in front for public-facing.

questions.scenario: A new application requires a queue with FIFO ordering, exactly-once processing, message group-based ordering (multiple groups process in parallel), and ~3000 messages/sec sustained.
questions.question: Which AWS messaging service fits?
choices: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with per-shard ordering — Kinesis's shard-based ordering provides FIFO semantics within a shard and the deduplication is handled by sequence numbers

Amazon SQS FIFO queue with high-throughput mode enabled (3000+ messages/sec per queue with multiple message groups), exactly-once delivery, deduplication by content hash or explicit ID, message group ID for ordering scope; pair with DLQ for poison messages
Amazon EventBridge with archive and replay — EventBridge's archive provides exactly-once delivery and the replay feature handles duplicate suppression for ordered event streams
Amazon SNS with FIFO topics — SNS FIFO provides ordered delivery with exactly-once semantics and scales to higher throughput than SQS FIFO via fan-out subscribers
questions.explanation:
SQS FIFO meets the requirements precisely. Ordering: strict FIFO within each MessageGroupId; messages with the same group ID processed in order. Parallel groups: different MessageGroupId values process in parallel. Exactly-once via deduplication (MessageDeduplicationId provided explicitly or content-based hash for 5 minutes). Throughput: high-throughput mode supports 3000+ messages/sec per queue (regular FIFO is 300/sec). Use cases: financial transactions, order processing, command sequences.

Kinesis with one shard caps at 1000 records/sec ingest, 2 MB/sec — not 3000 sustained; adding shards loses strict ordering. SNS Standard isn't FIFO; even SNS FIFO is for fanout, not strict consumer-side ordering. EventBridge isn't a queue and doesn't have FIFO semantics. The trap: candidates may know SQS Standard's higher throughput and assume FIFO is always slow.
questions.pattern: Queue/stream selection: SQS Standard (highest throughput, no order). SQS FIFO (ordered, dedupe). SNS (pub/sub). Kinesis Streams (ordered per shard, replay). MSK (Kafka, exactly-once with transactions). EventBridge (event routing). Pick by ordering, throughput, replay, fanout requirements.

questions.scenario: A new multi-tenant SaaS lets customers provide their own KMS keys to encrypt their data ('bring your own key' BYOK), with the customer retaining the ability to revoke access (rendering data inaccessible).
questions.question: Which AWS architecture supports BYOK with revocable customer-controlled keys?
choices: Customer-managed KMS keys in the CUSTOMER's AWS account, with key policy granting your application's IAM role permission to encrypt/decrypt; the customer can revoke by updating their key policy (immediate effect on data accessibility); pair with AWS RAM cross-account sharing patterns for centrally-managed application access
AWS-managed KMS keys with the customer specified as a delegated administrator — AWS-managed keys support BYOK semantics through the delegated-admin role granting revocation authority to the customer
Client-side encryption with the customer's RSA key pair stored in AWS Secrets Manager — Secrets Manager's per-secret access policies allow the customer to revoke access by deleting the secret

Customer-managed KMS keys in your application's account with the customer providing the key material via Import Key Material — once imported, the customer cannot revoke the key without contacting AWS Support
questions.explanation:
True BYOK requires the customer to OWN the key (not your account). Architectures: (1) Cross-account KMS — customer creates CMK in their account; key policy grants your application's IAM role kms:Encrypt/kms:Decrypt. Your app assumes a role and uses the customer's key. Customer can revoke by removing the policy grant — takes effect for new requests immediately. (2) AWS KMS External Key Store (XKS) — keys remain in customer-controlled external HSM (outside AWS); KMS proxies operations; ultimate customer control (key never leaves customer infrastructure). (3) Envelope encryption — data encrypted with per-object data keys, only data keys round-trip through KMS; high throughput, low KMS API cost. Critical caveat: AWS-managed and customer-managed-in-your-account keys are NOT true BYOK; they don't satisfy 'customer retains revocation control.'

Keys in your account = customer can't actually revoke. Bcrypt-of-passwords for data encryption is broken and isn't BYOK. CloudHSM in your account also isn't customer-controlled. The trap: candidates conflate 'customer managed' with 'customer-owned-in-customer-account'.
questions.pattern: BYOK levels: BYOK (customer-managed key, in customer or provider account). HYOK (hold-your-own-key, customer fully controls; AWS XKS supports this). Envelope encryption mandatory at scale (KMS API limits). Customer revocation mechanism (key policy update or external store disconnect). Audit via CloudTrail in both customer and provider accounts.

questions.scenario: A new global application has users in 6 regions. The team wants the SAME deployment artifacts deployed to every region with consistent IaC, automated multi-region rollouts, and per-region observability.
questions.question: Which approach is APPROPRIATE for multi-region deployments?
choices: Use AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM) to share infrastructure resources across regions — RAM's cross-region resource share automatically deploys equivalent resources in every shared region

CloudFormation StackSets (or Terraform with workspaces, or CDK with multi-environment deployments) — define infrastructure once, deploy as a stack instance per region/account; CodePipeline with cross-region/cross-account actions for orchestration; centralize artifacts in a single S3 bucket with cross-region replication; use AWS Service Catalog AppRegistry to track applications across regions
Configure AWS Backup with cross-region copy for all infrastructure — AWS Backup's infrastructure-replication mode duplicates the deployment configuration including IAM roles and networking
Manually deploy each region's infrastructure via the AWS Console — manual deployment guarantees that any region-specific differences are intentional rather than templated
questions.explanation:
Multi-region deployment automation: CloudFormation StackSets define once and deploy as instances to multiple accounts/regions; service-managed via Organizations or self-managed; supports drift detection. Terraform with workspaces or per-region modules — same pattern, multi-cloud-friendly. CDK programmatic multi-environment via stages; pipelines synthesize per-region stacks. CodePipeline cross-region actions orchestrate rollout: deploy to canary region → verify → propagate to remaining regions → optional auto-rollback. Per-region observability via CloudWatch metrics/logs/alarms; central dashboard via Cross-Account Observability. Traffic management via Route 53 latency/geo/weighted/failover routing; Global Accelerator for anycast IP. Data replication via DynamoDB Global Tables, Aurora Global, S3 CRR, ECR replication.

Manual deployment is error-prone. A single CloudFormation stack is per-region — you cannot reference resources across regions in one stack template. Single-region with VPC peering defeats the global low-latency goal. The trap: candidates may not know StackSets vs single stack distinction.
questions.pattern: Multi-region deployment: StackSets / Terraform / CDK for IaC. Pipelines for rollout. Observability per region + central. Traffic with Route 53 / Global Accelerator. Data replication. Test failover regularly.

questions.scenario: A new web application needs to handle authentication for 10 million users, with social login (Google, Apple, Facebook), MFA, password policies, custom user attributes, and the ability to migrate users from a legacy system without password resets.
questions.question: Which AWS service is APPROPRIATE?
choices: Amazon Cognito User Pools — managed user directory at scale, social IdP federation (Google/Apple/Facebook/SAML/OIDC), MFA (TOTP, SMS), password policies, custom attributes, hosted UI or custom UI via SDK, JWT token issuance, integrates with API Gateway/AppSync/ALB for backend auth; Cognito Identity Pools for federated AWS credential issuance
AWS Directory Service Managed Microsoft AD — Managed AD scales to millions of consumer users and provides social-IdP federation, MFA, and JWT issuance for any consumer-facing application

AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) users with one IAM user per end user — IAM scales to millions of end users and provides the password policies, MFA, and JWT issuance required for consumer applications
AWS IAM Identity Center with the consumer-application mode enabled — Identity Center is the AWS-recommended consumer authentication service that handles social IdP federation and JWT issuance
questions.explanation:
Amazon Cognito User Pools is purpose-built for application user management at scale. Directory: millions of users; per-user-pool config. Social federation: Google, Apple, Facebook, Amazon, plus SAML 2.0 and OIDC providers. MFA: TOTP (authenticator apps), SMS, optional/required. Password policies: complexity rules, lockout, history. Custom attributes: application-specific user data. UI: hosted UI (quick start) or custom via SDK/Amplify. User migration Lambda trigger: on first login, validate password against legacy; if successful, import user without forcing password reset. JWT issuance: ID and access tokens; integrate with API Gateway / AppSync. Cognito Identity Pools (separate) for temp AWS credentials for authenticated users.

Rolling your own auth is risky and expensive (security pitfalls, password resets, MFA flows, social login complexity). WorkSpaces is virtual desktops, not user-auth-for-applications. IAM Identity Center is for AWS console/CLI workforce SSO, not end-user app auth. The trap: candidates may confuse IAM Identity Center (workforce) with Cognito (end users).
questions.pattern: Identity service selection: end-user-of-application ⇒ Cognito User Pools. AWS-workforce-SSO ⇒ IAM Identity Center. Active Directory integration ⇒ AWS Directory Service / AD Connector. B2B partner federation ⇒ Cognito or custom OIDC. Mobile/web apps ⇒ Cognito + Amplify Auth.

questions.scenario: A new content-delivery use case: serve hundreds of thousands of small static images at the lowest cost; latency matters but budgets are very tight. Most users are global.
questions.question: Which AWS architecture MOST optimizes cost for global static-content delivery?
choices: S3 origin behind CloudFront with appropriate cache TTLs (long for immutable content, hashed filenames for versioning); CloudFront price classes (limit edge locations to US/EU only if cost > performance); compress text content (Brotli, gzip); HTTP/2 and HTTP/3; Origin Shield for cache-fill efficiency; consider S3 Intelligent-Tiering for the origin storage

Replicate the static content to S3 buckets in every AWS region and use Route 53 latency-based routing for delivery — multiple regional buckets provide the same global delivery as CloudFront at lower cost
Serve static content directly from S3 with public-read ACLs — direct S3 access is more cost-effective than CloudFront and S3's built-in CDN handles global delivery automatically
Use AWS Global Accelerator with TCP listeners pointing to a single S3 bucket — Global Accelerator's anycast network provides equivalent global delivery to CloudFront at lower per-byte cost
questions.explanation:
Cost-optimized global static delivery: CloudFront + S3 — CDN with global edge POPs absorbs traffic; S3 origin is cheap durable storage. Cache TTLs: long for immutable content (use hashed filenames for versioning, e.g., app.abc123.js); cache hits are free vs origin requests. Price classes: CloudFront has price classes (All / 200 / 100); limiting to US/EU saves cost if APAC traffic isn't important. Origin Shield: extra regional cache layer in front of origin; reduces origin requests. S3 Intelligent-Tiering: automatically tiers to cheaper storage classes for cold content. Compression: CloudFront compresses on-the-fly. HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 included.

EC2 fleet for static content is expensive (always-on instances, EBS, ALB). Lambda@Edge per-request cost is high for plain static content. DynamoDB for static blobs is wildly more expensive than S3. The trap: candidates may know each service but not the cost-comparison reality (S3+CloudFront is usually 10-100x cheaper for static).
questions.pattern: Static content cost: S3 + CloudFront is the default. Optimize: long TTLs, hashed filenames, price class limits, Origin Shield, Intelligent-Tiering, compression. For dynamic edge: Lambda@Edge or CloudFront Functions (cheaper for very simple logic).
